source: Adapted from BCT Taxonomy v1 (Michie et al., 2013) with South Asian development extensions

categories.name: Goals & Planning
categories.description: Techniques involving goal setting, action planning, problem solving, and behavioral contracts

techniques.name: Goal Setting (Behavior)
techniques.definition: Set or agree on a goal defined in terms of the behavior to be achieved
techniques.example: ASHA worker helps a pregnant woman set a goal to attend all four antenatal care visits at the sub-centre
techniques.southAsianContext: Widely used in ICDS and NHM programs where frontline workers help families set specific health behaviors. SHG facilitators use goal-setting for savings and enterprise targets. Effective when goals are framed around family wellbeing rather than individual achievement.
implementationTips: Use visual goal cards in local language
Frame goals around family/community benefit
Set short-term achievable milestones
Use SHG meetings as goal-review forums

caseStudies.title: Maternal Diet Goal Setting in Poshan Abhiyaan, Madhya Pradesh
caseStudies.description: Under Poshan Abhiyaan in Shivpuri district, AWWs helped pregnant women set specific weekly dietary goals - consuming iron-rich foods (green leafy vegetables, jaggery) at least 4 days per week and adding one egg or legume serving daily - documented on visual goal cards kept in the home.
caseStudies.outcome: Pregnant women with personalized diet goal cards consumed iron-rich foods 3.8 days/week compared to 2.1 days in the standard counseling group; maternal anemia prevalence decreased by 18 percentage points.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Poshan Abhiyaan Jan Andolan (People's Movement) nutrition interventions, Madhya Pradesh

techniques.name: Goal Setting (Outcome)
techniques.definition: Set or agree on a goal defined in terms of a positive outcome of wanted behavior
techniques.example: A mother in a Poshan Abhiyaan program sets the goal of her child reaching normal weight-for-age by the next growth monitoring session
techniques.southAsianContext: Used in nutrition programs (ICDS, Poshan Abhiyaan) where Anganwadi workers help mothers set child growth outcomes. Also applied in agricultural extension where farmers set yield targets. Works best when outcomes are measurable and culturally meaningful.
implementationTips: Link outcomes to growth monitoring charts
Use pictorial outcome cards for low-literacy populations
Connect outcomes to existing government scheme benefits

caseStudies.title: Child Growth Outcome Goal Setting in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In BRAC's community nutrition program in Rangpur division, community health workers helped mothers of underweight children set monthly weight gain targets using color-coded growth monitoring cards, with green (adequate), yellow (caution), and red (urgent) zones.
caseStudies.outcome: Children whose mothers set specific weight targets showed 40% faster recovery from underweight status; goal-setting was most effective when combined with fortnightly home visits and supplementary food support.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from BRAC community-based nutrition program evaluations, Rangpur

techniques.name: Problem Solving
techniques.definition: Analyse or prompt the person to analyse factors influencing their behavior and generate or select strategies that include overcoming barriers
techniques.example: During a Village Health Nutrition Day, facilitator helps mothers identify barriers to exclusive breastfeeding and develop strategies to overcome family pressure for early weaning
techniques.southAsianContext: Critical in contexts where behavior change faces structural barriers like poverty, caste, and gender norms. PLA (Participatory Learning and Action) groups in Bihar and Jharkhand have shown significant reductions in neonatal mortality through community problem-solving approaches.
implementationTips: Use participatory tools like problem trees
Address structural barriers alongside individual ones
Facilitate in mixed-gender groups where appropriate
Document solutions for peer sharing

caseStudies.title: Community-Led Problem Solving for Arsenic-Safe Water in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In arsenic-affected districts of Jessore and Satkhira, community groups used participatory problem-solving to identify barriers to switching from contaminated tubewells. Groups mapped safe and unsafe wells, negotiated shared access, and developed seasonal strategies for rainwater harvesting.
caseStudies.outcome: 68% of households switched to arsenic-safe water sources within 18 months; community-designed solutions showed higher sustained adoption than top-down approaches.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from UNICEF Bangladesh Arsenic Mitigation Program evaluations

techniques.name: Action Planning
techniques.definition: Prompt detailed planning of performance of the behavior including context, frequency, duration, or intensity
techniques.example: ANM helps a family plan the specific day, transport arrangement, and accompanying person for institutional delivery at the nearest PHC
techniques.southAsianContext: Essential for behaviors requiring logistical planning in resource-poor settings. Janani Suraksha Yojana beneficiaries benefit from detailed birth preparedness plans. WASH programs use action planning for toilet construction timelines. Must account for seasonal migration, agricultural calendars, and festival periods.
implementationTips: Include contingency plans for monsoon/harvest season
Involve family decision-makers in planning
Use pictorial planners for low-literacy contexts
Align with government scheme timelines

caseStudies.title: Household Toilet Construction Planning in Swachh Bharat Mission
caseStudies.description: In Madhya Pradesh's Dhar district, WASH facilitators helped households create detailed action plans for toilet construction including mason selection, material procurement timeline, pit design, and a savings plan aligned with MGNREGA wage cycles.
caseStudies.outcome: Villages with structured action planning achieved 85% toilet completion rates versus 52% in comparison villages; action plans reduced average construction time from 6 months to 8 weeks.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Swachh Bharat Mission Phase I district evaluations, Madhya Pradesh

caseStudies.title: Nutrition Action Planning Through VHND Platforms in Odisha
caseStudies.description: AWWs in Odisha's Rayagada district used Village Health Nutrition Days to develop weekly meal plans with mothers of malnourished children, mapping locally available foods to nutrition needs and creating step-by-step complementary feeding schedules.
caseStudies.outcome: Children enrolled in structured meal planning showed 40% faster recovery from moderate acute malnutrition compared to standard counseling; mothers reported higher confidence in feeding practices.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from UNICEF-supported VHND strengthening program, Odisha

techniques.name: Review Behavior Goals
techniques.definition: Review and reconsider previously set behavioral goals and modify if needed
techniques.example: Monthly SHG meetings where women review their savings and micro-enterprise goals and adjust targets based on seasonal income
techniques.southAsianContext: SHG federated structures provide natural review points. ASHA home visits serve as behavior review checkpoints in NHM. Agricultural Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) seasonal reviews help farmers adjust crop management practices. Review works best when integrated into existing community meeting structures.
implementationTips: Integrate into existing SHG/VHC meeting agendas
Use visual trackers for progress review
Celebrate incremental progress publicly

techniques.name: Review Outcome Goals
techniques.definition: Review and reconsider previously set outcome goals and modify if needed
techniques.example: Quarterly growth monitoring review where AWW and mother assess child's growth trajectory and adjust feeding goals accordingly
techniques.southAsianContext: Poshan Abhiyaan's growth monitoring framework creates natural review cycles. PM-KISAN beneficiary reviews help adjust agricultural outcome targets. Works well with community-based monitoring systems where group review provides social reinforcement.
implementationTips: Use growth monitoring as review trigger
Link to scheme benefit cycles
Share outcome data visually with communities

caseStudies.title: Quarterly Nutrition Outcome Reviews in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In Bardiya district under the Suaahara II program, Female Community Health Volunteers conducted quarterly growth monitoring reviews with mothers' groups, assessing children's progress toward growth targets and adjusting dietary recommendations based on seasonal food availability.
caseStudies.outcome: Communities with structured quarterly reviews showed 28% lower rates of growth faltering; the seasonal adjustment component was critical for maintaining dietary diversity during lean periods.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Suaahara II multi-sector nutrition program, Nepal

techniques.name: Discrepancy Between Current Behavior and Goal
techniques.definition: Draw attention to discrepancies between a person's current behavior and their previously set goals
techniques.example: ASHA shows a family that their child has only received 2 of 5 scheduled vaccinations despite committing to full immunization
techniques.southAsianContext: Effective in immunization drives where tracking cards make gaps visible. Swachh Bharat Mission used toilet construction tracking to highlight gaps between commitment and action. Must be done supportively to avoid shame, especially in hierarchical community settings.
implementationTips: Use visual trackers to make gaps self-evident
Frame as supportive observation, not criticism
Present in private settings, not public forums
Pair with problem-solving to address barriers

caseStudies.title: Handwashing Gap Analysis in Nepal Schools
caseStudies.description: In Kavre district schools, facilitators used 'glo-germ' powder to show students the gap between their perceived handwashing thoroughness and actual germ removal, creating visible discrepancy between their beliefs and reality.
caseStudies.outcome: Proper handwashing with soap increased from 22% to 71% within 3 months; students reported the visual demonstration as the most compelling motivator for behavior change.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from WaterAid Nepal school WASH program evaluations

techniques.name: Behavioral Contract
techniques.definition: Create a written specification of the behavior to be performed, agreed by the person and witnessed by another
techniques.example: Open defecation-free (ODF) village declaration where households sign a community pledge to use toilets, witnessed by the Sarpanch
techniques.southAsianContext: Swachh Bharat Mission effectively used community-level pledges and ODF declarations. Gram Sabha resolutions serve as collective behavioral contracts. SHG loan agreements include behavioral commitments. Works well when connected to community honor and collective identity rather than individual obligation.
implementationTips: Use Gram Sabha resolutions as formal contracts
Include community witnesses and leaders
Make contracts visual and displayable
Link to community identity and pride

techniques.name: Commitment
techniques.definition: Ask the person to affirm or reaffirm statements indicating commitment to change the behavior
techniques.example: During a VHSNC meeting, community members verbally commit to ensuring no child in the village misses immunization
techniques.southAsianContext: Public commitment is powerful in collectivist South Asian cultures. Panchayat-level commitments carry social weight. Religious and community leaders' public endorsements serve as commitment amplifiers. Jan Andolan (people's movement) approaches in SBM leveraged mass public commitments effectively.
implementationTips: Leverage existing community assemblies
Use religious/community leaders as commitment witnesses
Create visual symbols of commitment (flags, wall paintings)
Follow up commitments with action support

caseStudies.title: Open Defecation Free Village Commitment Ceremonies in Bihar
caseStudies.description: Following CLTS triggering in Bihar's Muzaffarpur district, entire communities made public commitments to end open defecation through signed village declarations, displayed prominently at panchayat offices and school walls.
caseStudies.outcome: Villages using public commitment ceremonies were 2.3 times more likely to achieve and sustain ODF status compared to those relying solely on individual toilet subsidies.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from World Bank Water and Sanitation Program evaluations, Bihar

categories.name: Feedback & Monitoring
categories.description: Techniques involving monitoring behavior or outcomes and providing feedback

techniques.name: Self-Monitoring of Behavior
techniques.definition: Establish a method for the person to monitor and record their behavior as part of a behavior change strategy
techniques.example: Mother uses a simple pictorial feeding diary to track how many times she feeds her child complementary food each day
techniques.southAsianContext: Adapted for low-literacy contexts through pictorial diaries and wall charts. ICDS programs use mother-held growth cards. WASH programs use household monitoring charts for handwashing. Works well when monitoring tools are simple, visual, and family-visible.
implementationTips: Design pictorial monitoring tools for low-literacy users
Use wall charts visible to the whole family
Keep tracking simple - 1-2 behaviors maximum
Integrate into existing health cards like MCP cards

caseStudies.title: Household Water Treatment Tracking in Sri Lanka
caseStudies.description: In chronic kidney disease-affected areas of North Central Province, households used simple wall charts to track daily water boiling or filtration practices, marking each day they treated drinking water before consumption.
caseStudies.outcome: Households using self-monitoring charts maintained 78% daily water treatment adherence over 6 months versus 41% in non-monitoring households.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from WHO Sri Lanka safe water promotion programs in CKDu-affected areas

caseStudies.title: Growth Monitoring Self-Tracking by Mothers in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In BRAC's Alive & Thrive program in Sylhet division, mothers of children under 2 maintained pictorial food diaries, tracking daily dietary diversity using a simple color-coded chart with food group symbols.
caseStudies.outcome: Minimum dietary diversity for children 6-23 months increased from 23% to 52% in self-monitoring households; mothers demonstrated better recall and understanding of diverse diet requirements.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Alive & Thrive Bangladesh program evaluations

techniques.name: Self-Monitoring of Outcome
techniques.definition: Establish a method for the person to monitor and record the outcome of their behavior
techniques.example: Farmer monitors and records crop yield differences between plots using traditional vs. improved seed varieties
techniques.southAsianContext: Growth monitoring in ICDS is a classic example - mothers track child weight on growth cards. Farmer field schools use crop cutting experiments for outcome monitoring. SHG members track savings accumulation. Digital tools like mSakhi app support ASHA self-monitoring.
implementationTips: Use mother-held growth monitoring cards
Enable comparison with previous monitoring periods
Use mobile phone-based recording where feasible
Celebrate positive outcomes in group settings

caseStudies.title: Mother-Led Growth Monitoring in Sri Lanka
caseStudies.description: In Nuwara Eliya plantation sector communities, mothers were trained to use simplified mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) tapes at home to monitor their children's nutritional status monthly, recording results on wall-mounted growth tracking sheets.
caseStudies.outcome: Early detection of wasting increased by 55%; mothers using home MUAC monitoring sought treatment an average of 3 weeks earlier than those relying only on health facility-based growth monitoring.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from WFP Sri Lanka plantation sector nutrition programs

techniques.name: Monitoring of Behavior by Others
techniques.definition: Observe or record the person's behavior as part of a behavior change strategy
techniques.example: ASHA conducts home visits to monitor whether families are using the toilet built under SBM or practicing open defecation
techniques.southAsianContext: Frontline workers (ASHA, AWW, ANM) serve as primary behavior monitors in India's health system. Community health volunteers play similar roles in Bangladesh and Nepal. Must balance monitoring with trust-building - punitive monitoring undermines behavior change. Peer monitoring through SHGs is often more effective than top-down monitoring.
implementationTips: Train monitors in supportive rather than punitive approaches
Use peer monitoring through SHGs where possible
Combine monitoring visits with service delivery
Ensure privacy and confidentiality

caseStudies.title: Peer Monitoring for Handwashing Stations in Bangladesh Schools
caseStudies.description: Student 'hygiene monitors' in primary schools of Mymensingh division observed and recorded peer handwashing after toilet use and before meals using simple checklists, providing weekly reports to teachers.
caseStudies.outcome: Schools with peer monitoring maintained 83% functional handwashing stations and 74% observed handwashing compliance over a full academic year.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from UNICEF Bangladesh School WASH monitoring reports

techniques.name: Monitoring of Outcome by Others
techniques.definition: Observe or record outcomes of a person's behavior as part of a behavior change strategy
techniques.example: AWW measures and plots child's weight monthly on growth chart during Village Health Nutrition Day to track nutrition outcomes
techniques.southAsianContext: Monthly growth monitoring at Anganwadi centres is India's largest outcome monitoring system. Community-based monitoring of school attendance under Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan. Water quality testing under Jal Jeevan Mission serves as outcome monitoring for safe water behaviors.
implementationTips: Share monitoring results with families in accessible format
Use visual displays at community centres
Train frontline workers in growth chart interpretation
Link outcome monitoring to supportive counseling

techniques.name: Feedback on Behavior
techniques.definition: Provide informative or evaluative feedback on performance of the behavior
techniques.example: Supervisor provides feedback to ASHA on her counseling technique during a home visit observation, highlighting what she did well and areas to improve
techniques.southAsianContext: Feedback loops are weak in many South Asian programs - strengthening them dramatically improves outcomes. Community scorecards provide collective behavior feedback. Digital platforms like RCH portal enable feedback on health worker performance. Feedback works best when immediate, specific, and delivered by a trusted person.
implementationTips: Provide feedback immediately after observed behavior
Balance positive recognition with constructive suggestions
Use peer feedback in group settings
Create feedback channels for beneficiaries too

techniques.name: Feedback on Outcome
techniques.definition: Provide feedback on the outcome of the person's behavior
techniques.example: AWW shows mother that her child has moved from red to yellow zone on the growth chart after two months of improved feeding
techniques.southAsianContext: Growth monitoring feedback is central to Poshan Abhiyaan. Water quality test results provide outcome feedback in Jal Jeevan Mission. Crop yield comparisons in farmer field schools serve as powerful outcome feedback. Visual, immediate feedback is most effective in low-literacy settings.
implementationTips: Use visual growth charts with color zones (red/yellow/green)
Provide feedback in supportive family setting
Compare with previous measurements to show progress
Link positive outcomes to specific behaviors

caseStudies.title: Real-Time Growth Monitoring Feedback in India
caseStudies.description: In Bihar's Samastipur district, Common Application Software (CAS) enabled AWWs to provide immediate visual growth feedback to mothers during weighing sessions, showing the child's growth trajectory on a smartphone screen with color-coded alerts and automated dietary recommendations.
caseStudies.outcome: Real-time digital feedback increased mothers' understanding of their child's nutritional status by 63%; follow-up visits for growth-faltering children improved from 34% to 72%.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from ICDS-CAS (Common Application Software) implementation evaluations, Bihar

techniques.name: Biofeedback
techniques.definition: Provide feedback about the body using an external monitoring device
techniques.example: Community health worker uses pulse oximeter to show a TB patient their improved oxygen levels after consistent medication adherence
techniques.southAsianContext: Increasing use of point-of-care diagnostics at community level - glucometers for diabetes, BP monitors, pulse oximeters. Hemoglobin testing (HemoCue) in anemia programs provides instant biofeedback. Pregnancy test kits at sub-centre level. The visual/numerical nature of biofeedback transcends literacy barriers.
implementationTips: Use point-of-care devices that give instant results
Train frontline workers to explain readings simply
Connect biofeedback to specific behavior changes needed
Ensure device maintenance and calibration

caseStudies.title: Hemoglobin Testing as Nutrition Biofeedback in India
caseStudies.description: In Rajasthan's Udaipur district, portable hemoglobinometers were used during community health camps to provide instant anemia status feedback to adolescent girls and pregnant women, with color-coded result cards linking hemoglobin levels to recommended iron-rich food consumption.
caseStudies.outcome: Women receiving biofeedback on anemia status were 2.4 times more likely to consistently consume IFA tablets and iron-rich foods; anemia prevalence in the intervention group declined from 68% to 43% over 12 months.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Rajasthan Anemia Mukt Bharat program evaluations

categories.name: Social Support
categories.description: Techniques that involve providing social encouragement, practical help, or emotional support

techniques.name: Social Support (Unspecified)
techniques.definition: Advise on, arrange, or provide social support from friends, relatives, colleagues, or community
techniques.example: Mobilizing mother-in-law support for a young mother's decision to delay second pregnancy through family counseling
techniques.southAsianContext: In joint family systems, social support from elders (especially mothers-in-law) is critical for behavior change in younger women. SHG networks provide structured social support. Community platforms like Mahila Mandals offer collective support. Must identify and engage key family decision-makers.
implementationTips: Map family decision-making structures first
Engage mothers-in-law as behavior change allies
Use SHG platforms for peer support
Address gatekeepers who may block support

techniques.name: Social Support (Practical)
techniques.definition: Advise on, arrange, or provide practical help for performance of the behavior
techniques.example: SHG members arrange childcare rotation so mothers can attend health checkups or vocational training
techniques.southAsianContext: Practical support systems like SHG-based childcare, community grain banks, and transport pooling address structural barriers to behavior change. MGNREGA provides practical support (income) that enables other behaviors. Cooperative models in dairy (Amul model) provide practical market support.
implementationTips: Build on existing mutual-aid traditions
Use SHG infrastructure for practical support
Address transport barriers to healthcare
Create community resource-sharing systems

techniques.name: Social Support (Emotional)
techniques.definition: Advise on, arrange, or provide emotional social support for performance of the behavior
techniques.example: Peer support group for mothers of malnourished children provides emotional encouragement and reduces stigma associated with malnutrition
techniques.southAsianContext: Emotional support is crucial in stigmatized conditions - TB, HIV, mental health, malnutrition. Peer support groups like those in Positive Living (PLHIV) programs provide safe spaces. ASHA as 'friend, guide, and activist' model emphasizes emotional rapport. Community-based mental health programs in Nepal and India use peer counselors.
implementationTips: Create safe, non-judgmental group spaces
Train peer counselors from same community
Address stigma directly in support design
Include men in emotional support programs where needed

techniques.name: Peer Support Groups
techniques.definition: Create structured groups of people sharing similar challenges to provide mutual support, information exchange, and accountability for behavior change
techniques.example: Mothers of severely malnourished children meeting weekly to share feeding strategies, emotional support, and track collective progress
techniques.southAsianContext: Peer support model underlies SHGs, mother support groups, farmer interest groups, and patient support groups. NHM's Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Committees create peer support. Alcoholics Anonymous model adapted for substance abuse. HIV positive networks provide peer support. Peer support is more sustainable than professional counseling in resource-limited settings.
implementationTips: Form groups with shared challenges and backgrounds
Train peer facilitators, not professional counselors
Create regular meeting rhythm
Celebrate group achievements

caseStudies.title: Mother-to-Mother Nutrition Support Groups in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In Barisal division's Alive & Thrive program, fortnightly mother-to-mother support groups of 8-12 women with children under 2 shared feeding challenges, practiced food preparation together, and provided mutual encouragement, facilitated by trained community nutrition promoters.
caseStudies.outcome: Mothers in peer groups were 2.1 times more likely to practice optimal IYCF behaviors; minimum meal frequency adherence increased from 51% to 78%; peer group mothers also showed higher self-efficacy scores.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Alive & Thrive peer support model evaluations, Bangladesh

techniques.name: Community Champions/Influencers
techniques.definition: Identify, train, and support community members who naturally influence others' behaviors through their social position, credibility, or charisma
techniques.example: Swachhata Champion in each ward who promotes waste segregation, monitors cleanliness, and serves as first point of contact for sanitation issues
techniques.southAsianContext: Champions model used across programs - Poshan Sakhis, Swachhata Champions, Jal Sahiya (water champions), Pashu Sakhi (livestock), and Krishi Sakhi (agriculture). Effective because they combine insider credibility with trained knowledge. Must be selected by community, not imposed. Compensation/recognition sustains motivation. Champions from marginalized groups challenge existing power structures.
implementationTips: Let communities select their own champions
Provide ongoing training and mentoring
Create recognition and support systems
Select champions from diverse backgrounds including marginalized groups

techniques.name: Mother-in-Law Engagement Program
techniques.definition: Specifically target mothers-in-law as key behavior change agents given their influence over young women's health, nutrition, and reproductive decisions in South Asian families
techniques.example: Saas-Bahu (mother-in-law/daughter-in-law) joint counseling sessions at AWC where MILs learn about modern nutrition and ANC practices alongside supporting their bahus
techniques.southAsianContext: In South Asian joint families, mothers-in-law often control food allocation, health-seeking decisions, and infant care practices. Programs that exclude MILs often fail because MILs override young mothers' intentions. Inclusive programs engaging MILs as allies have shown better outcomes for breastfeeding, nutrition, and ANC attendance. Must respect MILs' knowledge while introducing evidence-based practices.
implementationTips: Position MILs as partners, not obstacles
Acknowledge their experience and knowledge
Use inter-generational dialogue format
Celebrate MILs who support positive practices

caseStudies.title: Dadi-Nani (Grandmother) Nutrition Engagement in Bihar
caseStudies.description: In Bihar's Gaya and Nalanda districts, targeted nutrition counseling sessions engaged mothers-in-law (dadi/nani) as key influencers, addressing their concerns about colostrum feeding, early complementary feeding timing, and dietary diversity during pregnancy, and enlisting them as nutrition allies rather than barriers.
caseStudies.outcome: Colostrum feeding increased from 52% to 81%; early initiation of breastfeeding improved by 34 percentage points; the approach was most effective in joint family households where mothers-in-law controlled food decisions.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from PATH and CARE Bihar nutrition program grandmother engagement components

categories.name: Shaping Knowledge
categories.description: Techniques that provide instruction, demonstration, and information about how to perform behaviors

techniques.name: Instruction on How to Perform Behavior
techniques.definition: Advise or agree on how to perform the behavior, including skills training
techniques.example: AWW demonstrates proper handwashing technique with soap to mothers at the Anganwadi centre using step-by-step instructions
techniques.southAsianContext: Frontline worker training under ICDS, NHM, and SBM emphasizes instructional delivery. Cooking demonstrations for complementary feeding are a mainstay of nutrition programs. Agricultural extension uses demonstration plots for instruction. Must be culturally appropriate and account for resource constraints (e.g., soap availability).
implementationTips: Use hands-on demonstration rather than lectures
Adapt instructions to locally available resources
Provide pictorial job aids for reinforcement
Train in existing group settings (AWC, SHG)

caseStudies.title: Tippy-Tap Handwashing Station Construction Training in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In rural Terai communities of Sarlahi district, WASH facilitators provided step-by-step instructions for building low-cost tippy-tap handwashing stations using locally available materials - plastic bottles, sticks, string, and soap.
caseStudies.outcome: 92% of trained households successfully constructed functioning tippy-taps; 6-month follow-up found 67% still in use, with many households having constructed improved second-generation versions.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from SNV Nepal rural WASH program reports

caseStudies.title: Complementary Feeding Preparation Demonstrations in India
caseStudies.description: Anganwadi Workers in Jharkhand's Dumka district provided hands-on cooking demonstrations for preparing age-appropriate complementary foods using locally available ingredients (sattu, jaggery, green leafy vegetables, eggs) during monthly VHND sessions.
caseStudies.outcome: Mothers receiving practical demonstrations were 3.2 times more likely to prepare diverse complementary foods compared to those receiving only verbal counseling; timely introduction of complementary feeding improved from 38% to 64%.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Jharkhand Nutrition Mission program evaluations

techniques.name: Information About Antecedents
techniques.definition: Provide information about antecedents that reliably predict performance of the behavior
techniques.example: Educating families about how storing food uncovered attracts flies and leads to diarrheal disease in children
techniques.southAsianContext: Environmental health education identifies triggers - stagnant water leading to mosquito breeding (malaria/dengue), improper food storage causing contamination, seasonal patterns of disease. Agricultural extension identifies pest triggers. Understanding antecedents helps communities prioritize preventive action within resource constraints.
implementationTips: Link antecedents to locally observable phenomena
Use seasonal disease calendars
Connect to traditional knowledge about cause-effect
Make antecedent-behavior links visible through demonstrations

techniques.name: Re-attribution
techniques.definition: Elicit perceived causes of behavior and suggest alternative explanations
techniques.example: Challenging the belief that diarrhea in children is caused by 'evil eye' and reattributing it to contaminated water and poor hygiene
techniques.southAsianContext: Many health behaviors in South Asia are influenced by traditional beliefs about disease causation - evil eye, humoral imbalance, supernatural causes. Re-attribution must be done respectfully, acknowledging cultural frameworks while introducing biomedical explanations. Effective programs integrate rather than dismiss traditional understanding.
implementationTips: Never dismiss traditional beliefs outright
Use demonstration to make causal links visible
Engage traditional healers as partners
Build on existing health-seeking frameworks

techniques.name: Behavioral Experiments
techniques.definition: Advise on how to identify and test hypotheses about the behavior, its causes and consequences, by collecting and interpreting data
techniques.example: Farmer field school where farmers test improved vs. traditional rice varieties on side-by-side plots and compare yields
techniques.southAsianContext: Farmer field schools across South Asia use experiential learning through controlled experiments. CLTS (Community-Led Total Sanitation) uses transect walks as behavioral experiments to see fecal contamination pathways. Water quality testing helps families experiment with and see effects of water treatment. The 'seeing is believing' approach is powerful in contexts where external information is distrusted.
implementationTips: Design experiments with locally available materials
Let communities draw their own conclusions
Use comparative demonstrations
Document results for sharing across communities

categories.name: Natural Consequences
categories.description: Techniques providing information about health, emotional, environmental, and social consequences of behavior

techniques.name: Information About Health Consequences
techniques.definition: Provide information about health consequences of performing or not performing the behavior
techniques.example: IEC materials showing how open defecation leads to fecal-oral contamination and childhood diarrhea, stunting, and death
techniques.southAsianContext: Health consequence information is the backbone of India's IEC strategy across NHM, ICDS, SBM. Must go beyond fear-based messaging to include positive health outcomes. Visual media (posters, flipcharts) at AWC and PHC are primary delivery channels. Radio and TV spots through Doordarshan reach rural populations.
implementationTips: Balance threat messaging with positive outcome information
Use local testimonials alongside health data
Adapt visual materials for local context
Ensure information is actionable, not just alarming

caseStudies.title: Fluorosis Awareness and Safe Drinking Water in Rajasthan
caseStudies.description: In fluoride-affected areas of Jaipur and Nagaur, community health workers used photo-documented cases of dental and skeletal fluorosis to communicate health consequences of untreated groundwater consumption, linked with information about defluoridation options.
caseStudies.outcome: Awareness of fluorosis risks increased from 34% to 89%; adoption of community defluoridation plants reached 73% in intervention villages.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from UNICEF India fluorosis mitigation program documentation

caseStudies.title: Stunting Consequences Communication in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In the Multi-Sector Nutrition Plan implementation in Banke district, Female Community Health Volunteers used growth charts and visual aids to explain how poor nutrition in the first 1000 days affects lifelong health, cognitive development, and earning potential.
caseStudies.outcome: Mothers exposed to consequences-based counseling showed 45% greater adherence to recommended infant and young child feeding practices; knowledge of stunting causes improved from 18% to 72%.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Nepal Multi-Sector Nutrition Plan evaluation reports

techniques.name: Information About Social and Environmental Consequences
techniques.definition: Provide information about social and environmental consequences of performing or not performing the behavior
techniques.example: Explaining how open defecation affects the entire village's reputation and how ODF status brings collective dignity
techniques.southAsianContext: Social consequences are powerful motivators in collectivist South Asian cultures. SBM successfully leveraged social consequences (shame, dignity, marriage prospects) for toilet adoption. Environmental messaging around crop burning in Punjab uses social consequence framing. Village-level consequences resonate more than individual ones.
implementationTips: Frame consequences at community level, not just individual
Use stories of social outcomes from peer communities
Connect to community honor and collective identity
Be careful with shame-based approaches - can backfire

caseStudies.title: Water Source Contamination Mapping in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In Barisal division, communities participated in mapping exercises showing how open defecation contaminates shared water sources, tracing fecal-oral transmission pathways through their local environment using participatory GIS and transect walks.
caseStudies.outcome: Communities that undertook contamination mapping showed 56% higher latrine usage rates; the exercise was particularly effective in shifting social norms around open defecation near water bodies.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from icddr,b WASH research programs, Bangladesh

techniques.name: Information About Emotional Consequences
techniques.definition: Provide information about emotional consequences of performing or not performing the behavior
techniques.example: Sharing how mothers who practiced exclusive breastfeeding felt proud and confident versus anxious when using formula
techniques.southAsianContext: Emotional narratives resonate strongly in South Asian communication traditions. Testimonial-based approaches where community members share emotional journeys are effective. Must consider gender dynamics - women's emotional experiences may be dismissed in patriarchal settings. Safe spaces for emotional sharing are essential.
implementationTips: Use peer testimonials for emotional resonance
Create safe spaces for emotional expression
Include men's emotional experiences in gender programs
Use narrative and story-based formats

techniques.name: Salience of Consequences
techniques.definition: Use methods to emphasize the consequences of performing or not performing the behavior
techniques.example: CLTS triggering walk where community members see flies moving between open defecation sites and their food, making contamination pathways vivid
techniques.southAsianContext: CLTS triggering is the most powerful example of salience techniques in South Asian development - communities physically walk through areas of open defecation to confront the reality. Water quality testing with visible contamination indicators makes invisible risks salient. Nutritional deficiency impacts made visible through growth charts.
implementationTips: Use experiential methods over verbal/written information
Make invisible risks visible through testing/demonstration
Create emotional impact through direct observation
Follow up salience with actionable solutions

techniques.name: Anticipated Regret
techniques.definition: Induce or raise awareness of expectations of future regret about performance or non-performance of the behavior
techniques.example: Asking parents to imagine how they would feel if their daughter could not get a job because they did not prioritize her education
techniques.southAsianContext: Anticipated regret is effective for irreversible decisions like child marriage, school dropout, and delayed immunization. Beti Bachao Beti Padhao campaign uses this - parents imagining their daughter's future. Must be used carefully to avoid inducing guilt in already stressed populations. Works best for decisions with long-term consequences.
implementationTips: Focus on positive future scenarios, not just negative
Use role models who benefited from the right decision
Be sensitive to families facing genuine resource constraints
Pair with practical support to enable action

techniques.name: Information About Others' Approval
techniques.definition: Provide information about what other people think about the behavior, particularly approval from valued others
techniques.example: Sharing that the village Sarpanch, school teacher, and religious leader all endorse girls' education beyond Class 10
techniques.southAsianContext: Approval from respected community figures carries enormous weight in South Asian social structures. Religious leaders' endorsement of immunization or family planning can shift community norms. Panchayat resolutions signal collective approval. Doctor/teacher approval is highly valued. Caste and community leaders can amplify or block approval signals.
implementationTips: Identify and engage locally respected opinion leaders
Use endorsements from multiple authority figures
Include religious leaders where health behaviors face religious barriers
Create visible displays of approval (wall paintings, banners)

techniques.name: Positive Deviance Approach
techniques.definition: Identify and learn from community members who achieve better outcomes despite facing the same constraints as their peers
techniques.example: Finding mothers in a malnourished community whose children are well-nourished, identifying their unique feeding practices (using small fish, adding oil to dal), and sharing these with other mothers
techniques.southAsianContext: Positive Deviance (PD) pioneered by Jerry and Monique Sternin in Vietnam, extensively applied in South Asia for nutrition (Save the Children India), WASH, and newborn care. PD is powerful because solutions come from within the community, using locally available resources. ICDS programs have used PD hearths (community nutrition sessions). Most effective when the 'positive deviant' practices are simple and affordable.
implementationTips: Identify positive deviants from similar socioeconomic backgrounds
Focus on specific behaviors, not just outcomes
Use community cooking sessions to share practices
Ensure solutions use locally available resources

caseStudies.title: Positive Deviance for Child Nutrition in Vietnam-Inspired Bangladesh Program
caseStudies.description: Adapting the landmark Vietnam positive deviance model, community nutrition workers in Mymensingh identified families where children thrived despite poverty, documented their distinctive feeding practices (such as adding small dried fish to rice, frequent small meals, active feeding), and organized neighborhood cooking demonstrations led by these positive deviant mothers.
caseStudies.outcome: Severely malnourished children in PD hearth sessions gained an average of 450g per month; 72% graduated from severe to moderate malnutrition within 2 months; the approach proved more culturally acceptable than externally designed dietary recommendations.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Save the Children Positive Deviance/Hearth model implementations, Bangladesh

caseStudies.title: Positive Deviance for WASH Practices in India
caseStudies.description: In Assam's flood-affected Dhemaji district, positive deviance inquiry identified families maintaining safe WASH practices despite challenging conditions - they used raised platforms for water storage, practiced point-of-use chlorination consistently, and maintained handwashing routines even during displacement.
caseStudies.outcome: Villages adopting PD-identified WASH practices showed 44% lower diarrheal incidence during flood season; PD-based WASH messaging was more credible because solutions came from community peers.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from UNICEF India WASH resilience programming, Assam

categories.name: Comparison of Behavior
categories.description: Techniques involving comparing one's behavior with others or social norms

techniques.name: Demonstration of the Behavior
techniques.definition: Provide an observable sample of the performance of the behavior for the person to aspire to or imitate
techniques.example: Experienced mother demonstrates proper breastfeeding position and latch to a first-time mother at the Anganwadi centre
techniques.southAsianContext: Peer demonstration is central to South Asian programs - cooking demonstrations for complementary feeding, handwashing demonstrations, farming technique demos in KVKs. 'Model mothers' and 'model farmers' who demonstrate desired behaviors serve as powerful influencers. Video-based demonstrations via mobile phones are increasingly used.
implementationTips: Select demonstrators from similar socioeconomic background
Use hands-on practice after demonstration
Create video demonstrations for wider reach
Identify and celebrate 'model' practitioners

caseStudies.title: Model Household WASH Demonstrations in India
caseStudies.description: In Chhattisgarh's Jashpur district, designated 'Swachh Doots' (cleanliness ambassadors) maintained model households demonstrating proper toilet use, handwashing stations, safe water storage, and menstrual hygiene management, hosting monthly open visits for neighboring families.
caseStudies.outcome: Villages with model households achieved ODF status 4 months earlier on average; neighboring households adopted an average of 3.4 out of 5 demonstrated WASH behaviors.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Swachh Bharat Mission model village documentation, Chhattisgarh

caseStudies.title: Positive Deviance Nutrition Hearth Sessions in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In Sunamganj haor areas, mothers of well-nourished children from poor families demonstrated their feeding practices in community 'hearth' sessions, showing how they used small fish, orange-fleshed sweet potato, and moringa leaves to achieve dietary diversity.
caseStudies.outcome: 76% of severely underweight children enrolled in hearth sessions achieved adequate weight gain within 3 months; practices were sustained by 62% of families at 12-month follow-up.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Save the Children Positive Deviance/Hearth model, Bangladesh

techniques.name: Social Comparison
techniques.definition: Draw attention to others' performance to allow comparison with the person's own performance
techniques.example: Sharing that neighboring village achieved ODF status and received the Nirmal Gram Puraskar while this village has not
techniques.southAsianContext: Inter-village and inter-district comparison is a powerful driver in South Asia. Swachh Bharat rankings, district health indicators rankings, and school performance comparisons all leverage social comparison. Must be used constructively - can create unhealthy competition or shame. Exposure visits to 'model villages' are a form of positive social comparison.
implementationTips: Use positive comparison (aspiration) over negative (shame)
Organize exposure visits to successful communities
Share district/block rankings publicly
Ensure comparisons are fair - similar resource contexts

caseStudies.title: Village-Level WASH Scorecards in Nepal
caseStudies.description: Ward-level WASH committees in Sunsari district publicly displayed comparative scorecards showing each ward's performance on sanitation coverage, handwashing facility availability, and water quality testing compliance relative to neighboring wards.
caseStudies.outcome: Wards exposed to comparative scorecards improved sanitation coverage by an additional 23 percentage points compared to non-scorecard wards; inter-ward competition accelerated ODF declaration timelines.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from RWSSP-WN (Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Project in Western Nepal) evaluations

techniques.name: Information About Others' Behavior
techniques.definition: Provide information about what other people are doing, particularly people similar to the target individual
techniques.example: Telling a hesitant father that 85% of fathers in his block have already enrolled their daughters in secondary school
techniques.southAsianContext: Social norms messaging is highly effective in collectivist cultures. Communicating that 'most people in your community do X' shifts perceived norms. UNICEF and Breakthrough have used this for gender norm change. Data from local surveys makes this more credible than national statistics. Must ensure the norm being communicated is accurate to avoid backfire.
implementationTips: Use local data for credibility
Frame as 'most people like you' rather than abstract statistics
Ensure the communicated norm is actually true
Pair with stories of specific individuals

categories.name: Associations
categories.description: Techniques using prompts, cues, and stimulus control to trigger or prevent behaviors

techniques.name: Prompts/Cues
techniques.definition: Introduce or define environmental or social stimulus with the purpose of prompting or cueing the behavior
techniques.example: Painting footsteps leading from the toilet to the handwashing station as a visual cue for post-defecation handwashing
techniques.southAsianContext: Visual cues are extensively used in South Asian BCC - painted walls near toilets, stickers on water storage containers, color-coded dustbins for waste segregation under SBM. Temple/mosque announcements serve as auditory cues for health campaigns. Calendar-based reminders for immunization dates.
implementationTips: Use locally meaningful visual symbols
Place cues at the point of decision-making
Combine visual cues with social cues
Refresh/maintain cues regularly

caseStudies.title: Handwashing Cue Placement Near Water Points in India
caseStudies.description: In Tamil Nadu's Dharmapuri district, bright painted footprints leading from toilet exits to handwashing stations, combined with mirror-and-soap placement at eye level, served as behavioral prompts for handwashing with soap after defecation.
caseStudies.outcome: Observed handwashing with soap after toilet use increased from 17% to 54% in households with cue installations; children showed the highest behavior change response to visual prompts.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from WaterAid India handwashing behavior research

techniques.name: Cue Signalling Reward
techniques.definition: Introduce or define a cue that signals the receipt of reward following performance of the behavior
techniques.example: Green flag on household indicating ODF compliance, signaling eligibility for SBM incentive disbursement
techniques.southAsianContext: Government scheme markers serve as cue-reward signals - BPL cards, Ayushman Bharat cards, MGNREGA job cards. SBM used village-level ODF flags. Gold stars on immunization cards cue reward of scheme completion. Digital payment confirmations via SMS serve as modern cue-reward signals.
implementationTips: Link visible cues to tangible rewards
Use scheme cards/flags as status symbols
Ensure reward delivery is reliable and timely

techniques.name: Reduce Prompts/Cues
techniques.definition: Withdraw gradually prompts or cues that have been used to prompt the behavior
techniques.example: Phasing out ASHA home visit reminders for immunization as mothers develop the habit of tracking their own vaccination schedule
techniques.southAsianContext: Important for program sustainability - moving from intensive frontline worker prompting to self-sustained behavior. SHG maturation models reduce facilitator involvement over time. CLTS programs phase out external triggering as community ownership develops. Digital reminders can be gradually reduced as habits form.
implementationTips: Create graduation pathways in program design
Build community capacity before withdrawing prompts
Monitor behavior after prompt reduction
Maintain minimal prompts for critical behaviors

techniques.name: Stimulus Control
techniques.definition: Remove or alter stimuli that encourage undesirable behaviors and add stimuli that promote desired behaviors
techniques.example: Relocating tobacco/gutka shops away from school zones and placing healthy snack vendors near schools
techniques.southAsianContext: Tobacco-free school zone policies alter commercial stimuli. Removal of open defecation sites through construction/landscaping under SBM. Replacing alcohol outlets near industrial areas with recreation facilities. Adding safe drinking water points to reduce purchase of sugary drinks. Must address livelihoods of displaced vendors.
implementationTips: Work with local government for environmental changes
Address livelihood impacts of stimulus removal
Combine with positive stimulus addition
Use panchayat resolutions for enforcement

techniques.name: Satiation
techniques.definition: Advise the person to engage in the undesired behavior repeatedly until the urge diminishes
techniques.example: Used in de-addiction programs where controlled exposure reduces craving response over time
techniques.southAsianContext: Limited application in South Asian development but used in some addiction treatment programs - tobacco cessation through controlled reduction, substance abuse rehabilitation at government de-addiction centres. Behavioral satiation techniques for compulsive behaviors in adolescent programs.
implementationTips: Use only under professional supervision
Not appropriate for community-level programs
Combine with counseling and support groups

techniques.name: Habit Formation
techniques.definition: Advise to perform the behavior in the same context repeatedly so it becomes automatic
techniques.example: Encouraging mothers to always wash hands with soap before cooking the evening meal, linking handwashing to the daily cooking routine
techniques.southAsianContext: Habit stacking - linking new behaviors to existing routines - is highly effective. Handwashing linked to prayer times in Muslim communities. ORS preparation linked to cooking rituals. Immunization linked to festival/market days. Agricultural practices linked to seasonal calendars. Leveraging existing daily routines reduces cognitive burden.
implementationTips: Identify existing daily routines to attach new behaviors to
Use consistent timing and context
Start with one behavior-routine link at a time
Leverage cultural and religious routines as anchors

caseStudies.title: Water Purification Habit Stacking in Sri Lanka
caseStudies.description: In Anuradhapura district, the WASH program linked water filtering to existing cooking routines - 'when you light the stove for morning tea, also fill the filter' - using context-dependent repetition to build automatic water treatment habits.
caseStudies.outcome: Households using habit-stacking approach maintained 71% consistent water treatment at 9 months compared to 38% with standard health education; the approach was especially effective among working women with time constraints.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from National Water Supply and Drainage Board behavioral programs, Sri Lanka

caseStudies.title: Egg-a-Day Habit for Child Nutrition in India
caseStudies.description: In Rajasthan's tribal districts under the Poshan Abhiyaan, AWWs helped mothers build a daily egg-feeding habit for children 6-24 months by linking it to the morning meal routine and providing egg-tracking calendars with stickers.
caseStudies.outcome: Daily egg consumption among target children increased from 12% to 58% over 4 months; children in the habit-formation group showed significantly better linear growth velocity.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Poshan Abhiyaan complementary feeding interventions, Rajasthan

techniques.name: Habit Reversal
techniques.definition: Prompt rehearsal and repetition of an alternative behavior to replace an unwanted habitual behavior
techniques.example: Teaching a family to store water in covered containers instead of open vessels, practicing the new storage routine daily
techniques.southAsianContext: Replacing traditional practices with evidence-based alternatives requires careful cultural sensitivity. Replacing ghutti (traditional herbal remedies for infants) with colostrum feeding. Replacing open burning with composting. Replacing flood irrigation with drip irrigation. The replacement behavior must be equally or more convenient.
implementationTips: Ensure replacement behavior is feasible and affordable
Respect cultural significance of replaced practice
Practice new behavior multiple times
Provide material support for transition

techniques.name: Counter-Conditioning
techniques.definition: Advise the person to associate the unwanted behavior with an unpleasant experience
techniques.example: Associating open defecation with visible fecal contamination of food through CLTS triggering exercises
techniques.southAsianContext: CLTS triggering is the primary example - creating disgust association with open defecation through walking through contamination sites and mapping fecal-oral pathways. Anti-tobacco campaigns associating gutka with mouth cancer images. Must be used ethically and not create excessive shame.
implementationTips: Use carefully to avoid shame and stigma
Pair with positive alternatives
Ensure community consent for triggering exercises
Follow up with practical support

techniques.name: Linking Behavior to Government Benefits
techniques.definition: Create explicit connections between desired behaviors and eligibility for government scheme benefits to reinforce compliance
techniques.example: Linking ANC registration and institutional delivery to JSY cash transfer, creating a direct behavior-benefit association that motivates health-seeking
techniques.southAsianContext: India's conditional cash transfers create behavior-benefit links: JSY (institutional delivery), PMMVY (ANC+immunization), SBM incentive (toilet construction). Ayushman Bharat links enrollment to health facility usage. Jan Dhan links account to DBT receipt. These associations are powerful when benefit delivery is reliable and timely. Unreliable benefits weaken the association.
implementationTips: Ensure reliable and timely benefit delivery
Communicate the behavior-benefit link clearly
Simplify the process from behavior to benefit receipt
Track whether benefits actually reach the target population

techniques.name: Social Norms Marketing
techniques.definition: Use marketing approaches to shift perceptions of what is normal and socially acceptable, creating new positive norms through strategic communication
techniques.example: Campaign showing that 'real men' in this district support their wives' decisions about family planning, using testimonials from respected male community members
techniques.southAsianContext: Social marketing organizations like PSI, DKT, and Janani have used norms marketing for family planning, sanitation, and health products in South Asia. The approach redefines 'normal' through media, social proof, and aspirational messaging. Most effective when based on actual emerging norms rather than entirely aspirational norms. Must avoid moral imposition.
implementationTips: Base campaigns on real emerging norms, not wishes
Use community members as norm representatives
Combine mass media with interpersonal channels
Monitor for norm internalization, not just awareness

categories.name: Repetition & Substitution
categories.description: Techniques involving behavioral practice, rehearsal, substitution, and graded tasks

techniques.name: Behavioral Practice/Rehearsal
techniques.definition: Prompt practice or rehearsal of the performance of the behavior one or more times in a context or at a time when not necessary
techniques.example: Mothers practice preparing ORS solution during AWC sessions before any child falls ill, so they are ready when diarrhea occurs
techniques.southAsianContext: Practice sessions are integral to South Asian health programs - ORS preparation practice, kangaroo mother care rehearsal, emergency birth preparedness drills. Agricultural training uses hands-on field practice. WASH programs include handwashing practice sessions. Practice is more effective than instruction alone in low-literacy contexts.
implementationTips: Schedule practice during regular community meetings
Use real materials, not simulations where possible
Provide corrective feedback during practice
Repeat practice until confident performance

caseStudies.title: Household Water Treatment Practice Sessions in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In Cox's Bazar refugee camps and host communities, WASH promoters conducted supervised practice sessions where households rehearsed chlorine tablet dosing, SODIS (solar disinfection), and safe water storage chain management in their own homes.
caseStudies.outcome: Households completing 3+ practice sessions had 82% correct water treatment technique at 3-month follow-up compared to 45% among those receiving only verbal instructions.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from ACF (Action Against Hunger) WASH programs, Cox's Bazar

techniques.name: Behavioral Substitution
techniques.definition: Prompt substitution of the unwanted behavior with a wanted or neutral behavior
techniques.example: Encouraging families to use zinc-ORS treatment instead of antibiotics for childhood diarrhea
techniques.southAsianContext: Critical for replacing harmful traditional practices - replacing pre-lacteal feeds with colostrum, replacing home delivery with institutional delivery, replacing chemical pesticides with IPM, replacing open burning with compost. Must ensure substitute behavior is accessible, affordable, and culturally acceptable.
implementationTips: Ensure substitute is equally convenient
Address cost barriers to substitution
Use peers who have successfully substituted
Frame as improvement, not rejection of tradition

caseStudies.title: Replacing Ultra-Processed Snacks with Nutrient-Dense Alternatives in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In Sylhet division, community nutrition workers helped mothers substitute commercial packaged snacks (chips, biscuits) given to young children with locally prepared nutrient-dense alternatives such as mashed banana with ground peanut, chickpea flour laddu, and seasonal fruit.
caseStudies.outcome: Daily consumption of ultra-processed snacks by children 6-24 months decreased from 71% to 34%; dietary diversity scores improved significantly as local nutritious foods replaced commercial snacks.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from SPRING/USAID nutrition social and behavior change programs, Bangladesh

techniques.name: Graded Tasks
techniques.definition: Set easy-to-perform tasks, making them increasingly difficult but achievable, until the target behavior is performed
techniques.example: Starting with one handwashing occasion per day (before the main meal) and gradually adding more occasions until all five critical times are covered
techniques.southAsianContext: Gradual behavior adoption works well when target behavior seems overwhelming. Dietary diversification programs start with adding one new food group before targeting full dietary diversity. Financial literacy starts with small savings before loan management. Toilet usage starts with children before whole family adoption.
implementationTips: Start with the easiest, most impactful behavior
Celebrate each step achieved
Allow flexible timelines for progression
Use visual progress trackers

techniques.name: Overcorrection
techniques.definition: Ask the person to practice the desired behavior beyond the point of proficiency
techniques.example: Health worker practices emergency referral protocol multiple times beyond initial mastery during training refresher
techniques.southAsianContext: Used in frontline worker training - repeated practice of emergency protocols, counseling techniques, and clinical procedures until automatic. ASHA training modules include repeated role-play of counseling scenarios. Agricultural extension workers practice soil testing procedures multiple times.
implementationTips: Use in professional training, not community programs
Combine with coaching and feedback
Focus on high-stakes emergency behaviors

techniques.name: Generalization of Target Behavior
techniques.definition: Advise the person to perform the wanted behavior in a variety of contexts
techniques.example: Encouraging handwashing practice not just at home but also at school, workplace, and public places
techniques.southAsianContext: Behavior often learned in one context fails to transfer. Handwashing learned at AWC must transfer to home. Nutrition knowledge from training must apply in daily cooking. Financial behaviors learned in SHG must apply to household management. School-based life skills must transfer to home and community settings.
implementationTips: Practice behavior in multiple settings
Discuss barriers in different contexts
Create cues in each setting
Involve family members across contexts

techniques.name: Behavior Stacking
techniques.definition: Layer new behaviors onto existing routine behaviors to create chains of positive practices without requiring separate motivation for each
techniques.example: Adding handwashing with soap to the existing routine of mothers washing utensils before cooking, stacking a new hygiene behavior onto an established daily habit
techniques.southAsianContext: Behavior stacking leverages existing daily routines common across South Asian households - cooking, bathing, prayer times, tea/chai breaks, and market visits. Nutrition programs stack dietary diversity onto existing cooking routines. WASH programs stack handwashing onto toilet use. Financial literacy stacks onto SHG meeting routines. Lower cognitive burden than creating entirely new habits.
implementationTips: Identify strong existing routines as anchors
Attach one new behavior at a time
Make the new behavior very easy to perform
Reinforce the stack through visual cues at the routine location

categories.name: Comparison of Outcomes
categories.description: Techniques using credible sources and comparative information to influence behavior

techniques.name: Credible Source
techniques.definition: Present verbal or visual communication from a credible source in favor of or against the behavior
techniques.example: District Medical Officer endorsing immunization safety and effectiveness during a community health camp
techniques.southAsianContext: Doctor's word is highly trusted in South Asia. Government endorsement (PM, CM, District Collector) carries authority. Religious leaders (Imam, Pandit, Granthi) can legitimize health behaviors. Celebrities in government campaigns (Amitabh Bachchan for Polio, Aamir Khan for Swachh Bharat) reach mass audiences. Credibility varies by audience - peer credibility often outweighs expert credibility.
implementationTips: Match source credibility to target audience
Use local leaders for rural audiences
Combine expert and peer credibility
Address misinformation from alternative credible sources

techniques.name: Pros and Cons
techniques.definition: Advise the person to identify and compare reasons for wanting and not wanting to change the behavior
techniques.example: Facilitating a discussion with farming families about benefits vs. costs of shifting from paddy to millet cultivation
techniques.southAsianContext: Balanced decision-making facilitation is important in agricultural extension, family planning counseling, and migration decisions. ASHA home visits for family planning use structured pros-cons discussion. Crop diversification programs discuss economic and nutritional trade-offs. Works best when facilitator remains neutral and respects family autonomy.
implementationTips: Let families generate their own pros and cons
Provide accurate information for informed decisions
Respect autonomy in final decision
Address perceived cons with practical solutions

techniques.name: Comparative Imagining of Future Outcomes
techniques.definition: Prompt the person to imagine and compare likely or possible outcomes of changed vs. unchanged behavior
techniques.example: Asking an adolescent girl to imagine her life at 25 if she continues education vs. if she marries at 16
techniques.southAsianContext: Powerful for decisions with long-term consequences - early marriage, education dropout, crop diversification, migration. Beti Bachao Beti Padhao uses comparative future imagining. Livelihood programs help youth imagine different career pathways. Must be hopeful and empowering, not anxiety-inducing. Visual storytelling (comics, videos) helps make futures vivid.
implementationTips: Use positive future visualization, not fear
Show real examples of both pathways from community
Include concrete details to make futures vivid
Combine with practical planning for desired future

techniques.name: Persuasive Communication
techniques.definition: Use structured persuasive messaging designed to change beliefs, attitudes, and ultimately behaviors through logic, emotion, or credibility
techniques.example: UNICEF-produced radio spots featuring a respected community elder explaining why polio vaccination is safe and necessary, addressing specific myths with clear evidence
techniques.southAsianContext: IEC (Information, Education, Communication) and BCC campaigns across South Asian governments and NGOs use persuasive communication. Mass media campaigns for immunization, sanitation, and nutrition. Increasing sophistication from one-way messaging to two-way dialogue. Social marketing approaches (PSI, Hathi brand) apply commercial marketing to social behaviors. Must counter misinformation effectively.
implementationTips: Test messages with target audience before scaling
Use multiple channels for reinforcement
Address specific myths and misconceptions
Combine mass media with interpersonal communication

categories.name: Reward & Threat
categories.description: Techniques using material, social, and self-incentives, as well as punishment-based approaches

techniques.name: Material Incentive (Behavior)
techniques.definition: Inform that money, vouchers, or other valued objects will be delivered if the behavior is performed
techniques.example: JSY cash incentive of Rs. 1400 for institutional delivery in rural areas motivates pregnant women to deliver at health facilities
techniques.southAsianContext: Conditional cash transfers are widespread - JSY for institutional delivery, SBM incentive for toilet construction, PM-KISAN for farming, MGNREGA for labor. Incentives work for one-time behaviors but may not sustain habitual change. Perverse incentives (building toilets for money without usage) are a documented risk.
implementationTips: Link incentives to verified behavior, not just infrastructure
Design to avoid perverse incentives
Consider graduated incentives for sustained behavior
Ensure timely and transparent disbursement

techniques.name: Material Incentive (Outcome)
techniques.definition: Inform that money, vouchers, or other valued objects will be delivered if the outcome is achieved
techniques.example: Nirmal Gram Puraskar cash award to panchayats achieving verified ODF status
techniques.southAsianContext: Outcome-based incentives include Nirmal Gram Puraskar, district health rankings with budget allocations, and results-based financing in health. Pay-for-performance for frontline workers linked to coverage outcomes. Risk: gaming and false reporting. Needs robust verification mechanisms.
implementationTips: Establish transparent verification mechanisms
Guard against gaming and false reporting
Combine group and individual incentives
Ensure equitable access to incentive conditions

techniques.name: Social Reward
techniques.definition: Arrange verbal or non-verbal reward if the behavior is performed, including expressions of approval and encouragement
techniques.example: Public felicitation of 'Swachhata Champions' at Gram Sabha meetings, with certificate and recognition
techniques.southAsianContext: Public recognition is highly valued in South Asian collectivist cultures. Village-level awards for health, sanitation, and education champions. SHG recognition events. Teacher awards for school performance. Community naming of infrastructure after champions (water tanks, community halls). Social media recognition increasingly used in urban programs.
implementationTips: Ensure recognition is public and visible
Include diverse recipients (women, marginalized groups)
Use community events like Gram Sabha for recognition
Create ongoing recognition, not just one-time awards

caseStudies.title: ODF Village Public Recognition in India
caseStudies.description: In Maharashtra's Nanded district, villages achieving ODF (Open Defecation Free) status received public recognition through felicitation ceremonies, ODF declaration boards, media coverage, and priority consideration for other government scheme benefits.
caseStudies.outcome: Social reward mechanisms contributed to Maharashtra becoming one of the first large states to achieve ODF status; 89% of recognized villages maintained their ODF status at 18-month verification.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Swachh Bharat Mission Maharashtra documentation and verification data

techniques.name: Self-Reward
techniques.definition: Prompt self-praise or self-reward if the behavior is performed
techniques.example: Encouraging mothers to acknowledge and celebrate their own effort in preparing diverse meals for their children despite limited resources
techniques.southAsianContext: Self-reward is counter-cultural in contexts where women are socialized to prioritize others' needs. Programs must explicitly build self-recognition capacity, especially for women. SHG savings milestones can be framed as personal achievements. Self-care messaging for frontline workers (ASHAs, AWWs) who often neglect their own wellbeing.
implementationTips: Normalize self-acknowledgment in group settings
Build into SHG/group meeting rituals
Address cultural barriers to self-praise for women
Model self-reward behavior in training

techniques.name: Non-specific Incentive
techniques.definition: Inform that a reward will be delivered contingent on performance but the exact nature is not specified
techniques.example: Announcing that families with complete immunization will receive 'special recognition and benefits' at the upcoming block-level health mela
techniques.southAsianContext: Used in government campaigns where exact incentives vary by state/district. 'Lucky draws' at health camps for fully vaccinated children. Mystery gifts at school enrollment drives. Can generate excitement but may lead to disappointment if rewards are perceived as inadequate.
implementationTips: Set realistic expectations
Deliver on promises to maintain credibility
Use as supplement to specific incentives, not replacement

techniques.name: Future Punishment
techniques.definition: Inform that future punishment or unwanted consequences will result from performance of an unwanted behavior
techniques.example: Warning that continued burning of crop residue will result in fines under the National Green Tribunal orders
techniques.southAsianContext: Regulatory enforcement is weak in much of South Asia but improving. Crop burning fines in Punjab/Haryana, plastic ban enforcement, building code violations, child marriage penalties under PCMA. Punishment messaging works best when enforcement is credible. Communities often view punishment as unfair if alternatives are not provided.
implementationTips: Only use when enforcement is credible
Always pair with positive alternatives and support
Avoid targeting the poorest who lack alternatives
Phase in gradually with warning period

techniques.name: Remove Aversive Stimulus
techniques.definition: Advise that removal of an unpleasant consequence will occur if the wanted behavior is performed
techniques.example: Explaining that building and using a toilet will eliminate the daily discomfort, danger, and indignity of walking to open fields for defecation
techniques.southAsianContext: Powerful for WASH behavior change - removing embarrassment, safety risks (especially for women), and inconvenience through toilet adoption. Health programs frame treatment adherence as removing pain/symptoms. Financial inclusion removes the aversive experience of being dependent on moneylenders.
implementationTips: Identify the most aversive current experience
Make the relief tangible and immediate
Use testimonials of relief from those who changed
Address gender-specific aversive stimuli

categories.name: Regulation
categories.description: Techniques involving pharmacological support, reducing negative emotions, and conserving mental resources

techniques.name: Pharmacological Support
techniques.definition: Provide or encourage the use of medications to facilitate behavior change
techniques.example: Providing nicotine replacement therapy through government quitline to support tobacco cessation
techniques.southAsianContext: Government tobacco cessation centers provide pharmacological support. Iron-folic acid supplementation through ICDS/NHM facilitates anemia management. Anti-TB medication through DOTS. Contraceptive provision through family planning programs. Access to pharmacological support is limited in rural areas; pharmacies serve as key delivery points.
implementationTips: Ensure supply chain reliability
Train frontline workers in basic pharmacological guidance
Address stigma around medication use
Integrate with counseling and behavioral support

techniques.name: Reduce Negative Emotions
techniques.definition: Advise on ways to reduce negative emotions that may be barriers to performing the behavior
techniques.example: Teaching stress management techniques to ASHAs experiencing burnout, enabling them to continue community health work
techniques.southAsianContext: Mental health is increasingly recognized as a barrier to behavior change. Frontline worker burnout affects program delivery. Maternal depression affects infant feeding and care practices. Farmer distress affects agricultural decision-making. Addressing negative emotions through counseling, peer support, and stress reduction enables other behavior changes.
implementationTips: Integrate mental health support into existing programs
Train peer counselors from community
Reduce program-induced stress on frontline workers
Create safe spaces for emotional expression

techniques.name: Conserving Mental Resources
techniques.definition: Advise on ways to minimize demands on mental resources to facilitate behavior change
techniques.example: Simplifying the MGNREGA application process to a single-page form so that illiterate laborers can access the scheme
techniques.southAsianContext: Bureaucratic complexity is a major barrier to scheme access in South Asia. Jan Dhan Yojana simplified bank account opening. Aadhaar-linked DBT reduces cognitive burden of multiple ID documents. Simplified food labeling for packaged foods. Choice overload in agricultural markets addressed through curated recommendations.
