implementationTips: Reduce form complexity and documentation requirements
Use single-window service delivery
Provide facilitation support at access points
Design for lowest literacy level

techniques.name: Paradoxical Instructions
techniques.definition: Advise the person to engage in an exaggerated form of the unwanted behavior
techniques.example: In a gender training workshop, asking men to exaggerate stereotypical male behaviors to the point of absurdity, creating self-awareness of rigid gender norms
techniques.southAsianContext: Used in gender training and street theatre (Nukkad Natak) where exaggeration of harmful norms creates humor and reflection. Drama-based approaches in South Asia often use paradox effectively. Must be facilitated carefully to avoid reinforcing the very behaviors being challenged.
implementationTips: Use only with skilled facilitators
Follow with structured debriefing
Ensure safe space for exploration
Not appropriate for trauma-related behaviors

techniques.name: Motivational Interviewing Techniques
techniques.definition: Use client-centered counseling approach that explores and resolves ambivalence about behavior change through empathetic, non-judgmental dialogue
techniques.example: ASHA trained in MI techniques exploring a mother's ambivalence about institutional delivery, acknowledging her concerns about hospital treatment while building motivation for safe delivery
techniques.southAsianContext: MI principles are being integrated into frontline worker training in India. ASHA training under NHM increasingly includes counseling skills beyond information delivery. MI approaches respect client autonomy - critical in contexts where directive approaches from authority figures dominate. Challenging to implement at scale due to training intensity required.
implementationTips: Invest in intensive MI training for counselors
Practice through supervised role-plays
Focus on empathy and open-ended questions
Avoid prescriptive or judgmental approaches

techniques.name: Stress-Responsive Programming
techniques.definition: Adapt behavior change messaging and expectations based on household stress levels, avoiding additional burden during crisis periods
techniques.example: During drought season, ASHA reducing nutritional counseling intensity for farming families in acute stress and focusing on emergency nutrition support and emotional care instead
techniques.southAsianContext: Poverty-related stress affects cognitive bandwidth and decision-making (Mullainathan & Shafir's scarcity mindset). Monsoon failures, COVID-19, communal tensions, and family crises reduce capacity for behavior change. Programs must adapt intensity and expectations during high-stress periods. Providing practical support during crises builds trust for behavior change messaging during stable periods.
implementationTips: Monitor community stress levels and adapt programming
Reduce BCC intensity during crisis periods
Provide practical support during stress
Resume behavior change messaging when stability returns

categories.name: Antecedents
categories.description: Techniques restructuring the physical or social environment to support behavior change

techniques.name: Restructuring Physical Environment
techniques.definition: Change or advise to change the physical environment in order to facilitate performance of the wanted behavior
techniques.example: Installing handwashing stations with soap at school entrances under Swachh Vidyalaya Abhiyan to make handwashing easy
techniques.southAsianContext: Infrastructure as behavior change enabler is central to many programs - toilet construction (SBM), piped water connections (Jal Jeevan Mission), solar lighting for evening study, improved cookstoves (PMUY). Environmental restructuring must be accompanied by behavior change communication; infrastructure alone is insufficient.
implementationTips: Combine infrastructure with BCC messaging
Ensure community ownership of infrastructure
Design for maintenance sustainability
Make the desired behavior the easiest option

caseStudies.title: Point-of-Use Water Treatment Integration in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In Kalikot and Jumla districts, the WASH program redesigned community water supply systems to include built-in filtration points and covered storage, making safe water the default option rather than requiring additional household-level treatment steps.
caseStudies.outcome: E. coli contamination in household drinking water reduced by 79% in restructured systems; sustained behavior change was higher when safe water was the default rather than requiring active treatment.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Helvetas Nepal rural WASH infrastructure and behavior programs

techniques.name: Restructuring Social Environment
techniques.definition: Change or advise to change the social environment to facilitate performance of the wanted behavior
techniques.example: Establishing village-level nutrition committees with representation from all castes to create social support for dietary diversity
techniques.southAsianContext: Social restructuring includes creating mixed-caste SHGs, establishing VHSNCs with equitable representation, forming school management committees with parent involvement, and creating safe spaces for adolescent girls. Challenging hierarchical social structures is essential but must be done incrementally to avoid backlash.
implementationTips: Ensure representation of marginalized groups
Build on existing social structures where possible
Address power dynamics explicitly
Create accountability mechanisms

techniques.name: Avoidance/Reducing Exposure to Cues for Unwanted Behavior
techniques.definition: Advise on how to avoid exposure to specific social and contextual cues that prompt the unwanted behavior
techniques.example: Advising families to keep tobacco products out of the home to reduce children's exposure and prevent initiation
techniques.southAsianContext: Tobacco control through advertising bans, point-of-sale restrictions, and plain packaging. Alcohol control through dry days and restricted outlets. Junk food marketing restrictions near schools. Open defecation site conversion to community gardens. Must address economic interests of those profiting from harmful cues.
implementationTips: Identify and map key environmental cues
Work with local government for regulatory action
Provide alternative livelihoods where needed
Use positive environmental design alongside restriction

techniques.name: Distraction
techniques.definition: Advise or provide something to divert attention from the unwanted behavior
techniques.example: Providing play-based activities and toys at immunization centres to distract children and reduce fear-based refusal
techniques.southAsianContext: Used in child health services to reduce procedural anxiety. Engagement activities at ANC visits to reduce dropout. Livelihood activities for adolescent girls to divert from early marriage pressures. Community sports and cultural activities as alternatives to substance use. Diversion must address root causes, not just symptoms.
implementationTips: Address root causes alongside distraction
Use culturally appropriate diversionary activities
Design waiting areas at health facilities as engagement spaces

techniques.name: Adding Objects to the Environment
techniques.definition: Add objects to the environment to facilitate performance of the behavior
techniques.example: Distributing tippy-taps (low-cost handwashing devices) to households without piped water access
techniques.southAsianContext: Low-cost technology provision is essential - tippy taps, water purification sachets, ORS packets, IFA tablets, condoms, mosquito nets, solar lanterns, improved seeds. Distribution through AWC, PHC, SHG networks, and fair price shops. Objects must be culturally acceptable, affordable to maintain, and locally repairable.
implementationTips: Ensure products are locally maintainable
Create supply chains for consumables
Demonstrate usage alongside distribution
Monitor actual usage, not just distribution

caseStudies.title: Soapy Water Bottles at Handwashing Points in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In rural Tangail and Manikganj, WASH programs distributed soapy water bottles (detergent mixed with water in repurposed bottles) placed directly next to tube wells and latrines, reducing the cost and effort barrier to handwashing with soap.
caseStudies.outcome: Handwashing with soap at critical times increased from 5% to 37% with soapy water bottle provision; the approach was 6 times more cost-effective than bar soap distribution alone.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from icddr,b and World Bank handwashing intervention trials, Bangladesh

techniques.name: Body Changes
techniques.definition: Alter body structure, appearance, or functioning to facilitate behavior change
techniques.example: IUCD insertion at health facility enabling long-term contraception without daily behavioral effort
techniques.southAsianContext: Long-acting reversible contraception reduces behavioral burden. Cataract surgery restores functioning enabling other productive behaviors. Corrective eyeglasses for children improve school performance. Prosthetics and mobility aids enable livelihood participation. Must ensure informed consent and follow-up care.
implementationTips: Ensure fully informed consent
Provide follow-up care and support
Address myths and misconceptions about procedures
Integrate with comprehensive counseling

categories.name: Identity
categories.description: Techniques leveraging self-identity, role models, and cognitive consistency to drive behavior change

techniques.name: Identification of Self as Role Model
techniques.definition: Inform the person that their behavior may be an example for others to follow
techniques.example: Telling a mother who successfully practiced exclusive breastfeeding that other new mothers in the village look up to her and learn from her
techniques.southAsianContext: The 'model mother', 'model farmer', 'champion ASHA' concepts leverage role model identity. SBM created 'Swachhata Champions'. Poshan Abhiyaan identifies 'Poshan Sakhis'. Community members who adopt behaviors early become natural role models. This elevates status while reinforcing behavior maintenance.
implementationTips: Select role models from similar socioeconomic backgrounds
Provide public recognition and platforms
Ensure diversity in role model selection
Support role models to share their stories

caseStudies.title: Father as Nutrition Role Model in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In Kapilvastu district under the Suaahara II program, fathers were engaged as nutrition role models, publicly demonstrating feeding young children, purchasing diverse foods from local markets, and attending growth monitoring sessions - traditionally seen as women-only activities.
caseStudies.outcome: Households with engaged father role models showed 33% higher dietary diversity for children; male involvement in child feeding increased from 8% to 42% in intervention areas.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Suaahara II father engagement component, Nepal

techniques.name: Framing/Reframing
techniques.definition: Suggest the deliberate adoption of a perspective or new perspective on behavior to change cognitions or emotions about it
techniques.example: Reframing toilet use from 'a facility for the house' to 'a matter of dignity and safety for women in the family'
techniques.southAsianContext: SBM successfully reframed sanitation from hygiene to dignity, particularly women's dignity. Nutrition reframed from 'feeding' to 'first 1000 days of brain development'. Girl child education reframed from cost to investment. Agricultural diversification reframed from risk to opportunity. Effective reframing connects to existing values and aspirations.
implementationTips: Connect to existing cultural values
Use language and metaphors that resonate locally
Test reframes with target audience first
Address the concern behind resistance, not just the behavior

techniques.name: Incompatible Beliefs
techniques.definition: Draw attention to discrepancies between current or past behavior and self-image to create discomfort that motivates behavior change
techniques.example: Pointing out to a father who values family honor that allowing open defecation contradicts his commitment to protecting his family's dignity
techniques.southAsianContext: Effective in contexts where honor (izzat), family reputation, and community standing are strong values. SBM used 'izzat ghar' (house of dignity) framing for toilets. Gender programs highlight contradictions between loving daughters and restricting their education. Must be done sensitively to avoid defensiveness.
implementationTips: Use respectfully and privately
Connect to values the person already holds
Provide clear pathway to resolve the discrepancy
Avoid public shaming

techniques.name: Valued Self-Identity
techniques.definition: Advise the person to write or complete ratings of valued self-identities to affirm their sense of self
techniques.example: SHG members articulate their identity as 'empowered women entrepreneurs' and 'community leaders' during federation meetings
techniques.southAsianContext: Identity affirmation is powerful for marginalized communities. Dalit women affirming their identity as 'leaders' in SHGs. Tribal communities affirming identity as 'forest guardians' in conservation programs. Youth affirming identity as 'changemakers'. Identity shifts drive sustained behavior change beyond specific program targets.
implementationTips: Create spaces for positive identity articulation
Use group processes to reinforce new identities
Link identity to collective action, not just individual
Document and celebrate identity narratives

techniques.name: Cognitive Dissonance
techniques.definition: Draw attention to inconsistencies between a person's attitudes and their actual behavior to create discomfort and motivate change
techniques.example: In a gender training, asking men who say they support women's equality why their wives are not allowed to work outside the home
techniques.southAsianContext: Widely used in gender transformative programs like Breakthrough's Bell Bajao and Population Foundation's Main Kuch Bhi Kar Sakti Hoon. Creates productive discomfort between stated progressive values and actual patriarchal practices. Must be facilitated carefully with skilled trainers to move from discomfort to action.
implementationTips: Use skilled facilitators only
Create safe environment for reflection
Move from dissonance to concrete action plans
Follow up to support sustained change

techniques.name: Narrative Identity Shift
techniques.definition: Help individuals and communities construct new narratives about who they are that incorporate the desired behavior as a core part of their identity
techniques.example: Tribal communities reframing their identity from 'backward' to 'guardians of traditional knowledge and biodiversity' through FRA-based forest management
techniques.southAsianContext: Identity shifts drive sustained behavior change. Dalit assertion movements reframe identity from 'oppressed' to 'empowered rights-holders'. Tribal communities from 'beneficiaries' to 'custodians'. Women from 'dependents' to 'entrepreneurs'. SHG movements create 'empowered collective' identity. Identity reframing must come from within the community, not imposed externally.
implementationTips: Support community-led narrative construction
Use documentation and storytelling for identity building
Create platforms for new identity expression
Connect individual identity to collective identity

techniques.name: Aspirational Identity Construction
techniques.definition: Help individuals envision and adopt an aspirational identity that naturally incorporates the desired behavior as part of who they want to become
techniques.example: Adolescent girls constructing 'future self' vision boards showing themselves as educated professionals, which naturally incorporates continued education as consistent with their aspirational identity
techniques.southAsianContext: Rising aspirations across South Asia, driven by media, urbanization, and education, create opportunities for aspirational identity-based BCC. 'Modern mother' identity incorporates health-seeking behavior. 'Progressive farmer' identity incorporates technology adoption. 'Empowered woman' identity incorporates financial independence. Must ensure aspirations are achievable to avoid frustration.
implementationTips: Help construct achievable aspirational identities
Use role models who achieved the aspired identity
Connect daily behaviors to long-term identity goals
Ensure aspirations don't create unhealthy social comparison

categories.name: Scheduled Consequences
categories.description: Techniques using systematic reinforcement schedules and consequence management

techniques.name: Behavior Cost
techniques.definition: Arrange for withdrawal of something valued if an unwanted behavior is performed
techniques.example: SHG group rule that members who miss three consecutive meetings lose their turn in the loan rotation
techniques.southAsianContext: SHG internal rules create behavior costs - fines for late savings, missed meetings, or loan defaults. School attendance linked to mid-day meal access. MGNREGA work attendance tracked for payment. Must be proportionate and not punish those facing genuine barriers.
implementationTips: Ensure costs are proportionate and fair
Distinguish between willful non-compliance and genuine barriers
Allow grace periods and appeals
Use community consensus for rule-setting

techniques.name: Reward Approximation
techniques.definition: Reward any behavior that approximates the target behavior, gradually requiring closer approximation for reward
techniques.example: Initially praising a family for having a toilet pit dug, then for walls built, then for regular use, with recognition at each stage
techniques.southAsianContext: Used in phased program implementation - SBM rewarded toilet construction stages, not just completion. Education programs recognize attendance improvements, not just 100% attendance. Nutrition programs celebrate any dietary improvement, not just complete dietary diversity. Recognizes incremental progress in resource-constrained settings.
implementationTips: Define clear milestones for recognition
Celebrate each stage of progress
Gradually raise expectations
Use visual progress trackers

techniques.name: Situation-Specific Reward
techniques.definition: Reward the behavior in one situation but not in another
techniques.example: Providing nutritious snacks to children who attend school (mid-day meal) while not providing them when absent
techniques.southAsianContext: Mid-Day Meal Scheme is India's largest situation-specific reward - nutrition linked to school attendance. Immunization camps with gifts for children who complete schedules. Health check-up camps with free medications. Take-home ration from AWC contingent on attendance. Must not deny essential services to non-compliant populations.
implementationTips: Never deny basic rights/entitlements as consequence
Ensure the reward is genuinely valued
Make the situation-reward link clear
Monitor for unintended exclusion of vulnerable groups

techniques.name: Reward Completion
techniques.definition: Build in a reward for completion of the behavior
techniques.example: Certificate and public recognition for mothers who complete all immunization visits for their child under Mission Indradhanush
techniques.southAsianContext: Completion certificates under Mission Indradhanush. SHG loan completion recognition. Course completion certificates on ImpactMojo. Full immunization cards as completion markers. Village-level celebrations for achieving ODF or full immunization coverage. Completion rewards reinforce the full behavior cycle.
implementationTips: Make completion visible and celebratory
Use certificates, badges, or community events
Recognize completion at community forums
Link completion to next-level opportunities

categories.name: Self-Belief
categories.description: Techniques building confidence, self-efficacy, and belief in one's ability to change

techniques.name: Verbal Persuasion About Capability
techniques.definition: Tell the person that they can successfully perform the behavior, arguing against self-doubts
techniques.example: ASHA reassuring a first-time mother that she is capable of breastfeeding exclusively and that initial difficulties are normal and manageable
techniques.southAsianContext: Self-efficacy building is critical for marginalized populations who internalize inability. Women in SHGs being told they can manage finances and run enterprises. Farmers told they can adopt new technologies. Youth from disadvantaged backgrounds told they can succeed academically. Frontline workers' motivation depends on belief in their own capability.
implementationTips: Use peer examples of success from similar backgrounds
Address specific self-doubts, not general encouragement
Provide skill-building alongside encouragement
Train frontline workers in motivational communication

techniques.name: Mental Rehearsal of Successful Performance
techniques.definition: Advise the person to rehearse the successful performance of the behavior mentally
techniques.example: Asking a rural woman to visualize herself confidently speaking at the Gram Sabha meeting before attending
techniques.southAsianContext: Visualization techniques used in women's leadership development, public speaking training for panchayat representatives, and farmer training for new techniques. Mental rehearsal helps overcome anxiety about new behaviors in formal settings. Used in adolescent life skills programs for assertiveness.
implementationTips: Guide visualization with specific details
Combine with actual practice opportunities
Address fears and anxieties explicitly
Use relaxation techniques alongside visualization

techniques.name: Focus on Past Success
techniques.definition: Advise the person to think about or list previous successes in performing the behavior or similar behaviors
techniques.example: Reminding an SHG that successfully saved and repaid previous loans, they have the capacity to take on a larger enterprise loan
techniques.southAsianContext: Builds on past achievements to motivate new challenges. SHG credit history demonstrates financial capability. Communities that achieved ODF status can tackle other development goals. Farmers who adopted one new technology can adopt more. Documenting and celebrating past successes creates organizational memory and momentum.
implementationTips: Document and display past achievements visually
Reference specific past successes, not general ones
Build organizational memory of community achievements
Use success stories in new challenge framing

techniques.name: Self-Talk
techniques.definition: Prompt positive self-talk before and during the behavior
techniques.example: Teaching adolescent girls affirmations like 'I deserve education' and 'My voice matters' to build confidence in claiming their rights
techniques.southAsianContext: Adapted for South Asian contexts through life skills programs for adolescent girls (SABLA, Kishori Shakti). Self-affirmation exercises in gender training. Frontline workers using positive self-talk before difficult household visits. Must be culturally adapted - collectivist framing ('we can do this') often more resonant than individual ('I can do this').
implementationTips: Adapt to collectivist cultural norms
Use group affirmation alongside individual
Integrate into regular meeting rituals
Connect self-talk to community identity

techniques.name: Mastery Experience
techniques.definition: Provide opportunities for the person to successfully perform the behavior or a simpler version of it, building confidence through direct experience of success
techniques.example: Women panchayat representatives first practicing public speaking in small SHG groups before addressing the full Gram Sabha, building confidence through graduated mastery
techniques.southAsianContext: Mastery experiences are the strongest source of self-efficacy. SHG progression from savings to credit to enterprise builds mastery. Women's leadership development through graduated responsibility. Farmer field school hands-on practice builds agricultural mastery. Must start with achievable challenges and build progressively.
implementationTips: Start with easy, achievable tasks
Provide supportive feedback after each success
Gradually increase difficulty
Celebrate each milestone

categories.name: Covert Learning
categories.description: Techniques using imagination, mental imagery, and vicarious experience for behavior change

techniques.name: Imaginary Punishment
techniques.definition: Advise the person to imagine performing the unwanted behavior and experiencing punishment
techniques.example: Asking a person to imagine the social embarrassment of being identified as practicing open defecation during a community verification visit
techniques.southAsianContext: Limited direct application but implicit in many South Asian BCC campaigns that evoke imagined negative scenarios. Anti-tobacco ads showing imagined illness. Anti-dowry campaigns showing imagined consequences. Must be used carefully as excessive fear imagery can be counterproductive.
implementationTips: Use sparingly and ethically
Balance with positive imagery
Avoid excessive fear appeal
Follow with practical action guidance

techniques.name: Imaginary Reward
techniques.definition: Advise the person to imagine performing the wanted behavior in a context where they would gain reward
techniques.example: Asking a mother to imagine the pride she would feel seeing her well-nourished child start school healthy and strong
techniques.southAsianContext: Positive visualization is embedded in South Asian storytelling and aspiration-building approaches. Programs help parents visualize their child's successful future. Women imagine their lives as empowered entrepreneurs. Farmers imagine prosperous harvests from improved practices. Aspiration-based approaches are more culturally resonant than fear-based ones.
implementationTips: Use vivid, culturally resonant positive imagery
Connect imagined rewards to deeply held aspirations
Use storytelling formats for guided imagination
Make the imagined future feel achievable

techniques.name: Vicarious Consequences
techniques.definition: Prompt observation of the consequences (reward or punishment) of others' behavior to influence behavior change
techniques.example: Showing a video of a neighboring village that received a government award and development funds after achieving total sanitation coverage
techniques.southAsianContext: Exposure visits to successful communities are the primary vicarious learning method in South Asian development. Farmer field days showing successful adopters' outcomes. Videos of model villages/programs. Success story documentation and sharing. Especially effective when the observed community is similar in socioeconomic profile.
implementationTips: Select communities similar in profile for exposure visits
Document both positive and negative examples
Facilitate reflection after vicarious observation
Create video libraries of success stories for wider reach

categories.name: Community Mobilization & Collective Action
categories.description: Techniques leveraging community structures, collective action, and participatory processes for behavior change at scale

techniques.name: Participatory Learning and Action (PLA) Cycles
techniques.definition: Facilitate community groups through structured cycles of identifying problems, planning, acting, and reflecting on health and social issues
techniques.example: Women's groups in Jharkhand meeting fortnightly to identify maternal health problems, plan solutions, and take collective action - resulting in 37% reduction in neonatal mortality
techniques.southAsianContext: PLA groups have shown dramatic impacts on maternal and neonatal health in trials across India (Jharkhand, Odisha), Nepal, and Bangladesh. The Ekjut trial demonstrated significant mortality reduction. Builds on South Asia's strong tradition of women's collectives. Now being adapted for nutrition (Poshan Abhiyaan) and WASH.
implementationTips: Use trained local women as facilitators
Follow structured 4-phase cycle
Meet regularly (fortnightly/monthly)
Ensure representation of marginalized women
Document and share learnings across groups

caseStudies.title: PLA Cycles for Total Sanitation in Nepal
caseStudies.description: Women's groups in Dhanusha and Mahottari districts used 10-meeting PLA cycles focused on WASH, identifying sanitation problems, analyzing causes through community mapping, planning collective action, and implementing and evaluating solutions including shared toilet construction and drainage improvement.
caseStudies.outcome: Wards with WASH-focused PLA groups achieved 34% higher sanitation coverage than comparison areas; women's agency in household WASH decisions increased significantly.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from NHSSP (Nepal Health Sector Support Programme) PLA documentation

caseStudies.title: PLA Groups for Infant Feeding in Jharkhand
caseStudies.description: In Jharkhand's East Singhbhum district, tribal women's groups used PLA cycles to address infant and young child feeding challenges, identifying cultural barriers to early initiation of breastfeeding, discussing prelacteal feeding practices, and developing community-generated solutions.
caseStudies.outcome: Early initiation of breastfeeding increased from 33% to 65%; exclusive breastfeeding at 6 months improved from 47% to 79% in PLA intervention areas, with concurrent reduction in neonatal mortality.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Ekjut PLA trial and scale-up, Jharkhand and Odisha

techniques.name: Community Scorecard
techniques.definition: Enable communities to rate and score the quality and accessibility of local services, creating accountability pressure for improvement
techniques.example: Community members in a block health meeting scoring their PHC on availability of medicines, staff attendance, and cleanliness, with results shared with district officials
techniques.southAsianContext: Used by CARE India, World Vision, and others for health facility accountability. Community scorecards in Bangladesh improved facility-based delivery. Adapted for PDS, ICDS, and school quality monitoring. Creates structured dialogue between service providers and communities. Must be facilitated to avoid elite capture.
implementationTips: Ensure inclusive participation across caste/class
Share results with decision-makers who can act
Follow up on action commitments
Build community capacity for ongoing monitoring

techniques.name: Social Audit
techniques.definition: Facilitate systematic public examination of government expenditure and service delivery by community members
techniques.example: MGNREGA social audit where village residents verify wage payments, measure completed works, and identify discrepancies publicly
techniques.southAsianContext: Mandated for MGNREGA and increasingly for other schemes. Andhra Pradesh and Telangana have institutionalized social audit societies. Meghalaya pioneered community-led audits. Exposes corruption, improves service delivery, and builds citizen engagement. Requires strong civil society support and political will.
implementationTips: Train community auditors systematically
Ensure legal protection for auditors
Present findings at public hearings (Jan Sunwai)
Follow up on audit recommendations

techniques.name: Gram Sabha Activation
techniques.definition: Strengthen village assembly meetings as forums for collective decision-making on behavior change priorities and resource allocation
techniques.example: Using Gram Sabha to pass resolution declaring village alcohol-free, with community-agreed enforcement mechanisms
techniques.southAsianContext: Gram Sabhas are constitutionally mandated but often dysfunctional. When activated effectively, they serve as powerful behavior change platforms - ODF declarations, alcohol prohibition, child marriage prevention resolutions, education commitments. PESA areas have additional powers. Women's Gram Sabhas (Mahila Gram Sabha) are mandated in some states.
implementationTips: Ensure quorum with marginalized group representation
Use structured agenda for behavior change topics
Record and publicize resolutions
Build capacity of Panchayat members to facilitate

techniques.name: Self-Help Group (SHG) Leveraging
techniques.definition: Use existing SHG platforms and networks to deliver behavior change messaging, create peer support, and establish social norms
techniques.example: Integrating nutrition education and cooking demonstrations into weekly SHG meetings in Bihar under JEEViKA program
techniques.southAsianContext: India has over 90 lakh SHGs reaching 10+ crore women under DAY-NRLM. SHGs are the most powerful community platform for BCC in rural India. JEEViKA in Bihar, Kudumbashree in Kerala, and SERP in Andhra Pradesh have demonstrated health, nutrition, and livelihood behavior change through SHG convergence.
implementationTips: Don't overload SHGs with too many BCC topics
Train community resource persons (CRPs) as facilitators
Use SHG federations for scale-up
Align with existing SHG meeting agenda

techniques.name: Community-Led Total Sanitation (CLTS) Triggering
techniques.definition: Use facilitated community analysis of open defecation practices to trigger collective disgust and motivate community-wide toilet adoption
techniques.example: Facilitator leads community through defecation area mapping, fecal-oral transmission pathway analysis, and 'glass of water' exercise triggering collective decision to end open defecation
techniques.southAsianContext: Originated in Bangladesh (Kamal Kar, 2000) and adapted across South Asia. Foundational to SBM's community approach. Uses shock and disgust to overcome normalization of open defecation. Criticisms include potential for shaming - evolved approaches emphasize dignity and positive motivation alongside triggering.
implementationTips: Use certified CLTS facilitators
Follow up triggering with practical toilet support
Monitor for coercive practices
Combine with positive motivation approaches

caseStudies.title: CLTS Triggering Adapted for Flood-Prone Areas in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: Standard CLTS triggering was adapted for char (riverine island) communities in Kurigram where annual flooding destroys conventional latrines. Triggering sessions included calculation of fecal contamination in flood waters and co-design of raised and flood-resilient latrine models.
caseStudies.outcome: Adapted CLTS in flood-prone areas achieved 54% sustained latrine use compared to 23% with standard approaches; community-designed raised latrines survived 2 consecutive flood seasons.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Practical Action and WaterAid CLTS adaptation programs, northern Bangladesh

techniques.name: Jan Sunwai (Public Hearing)
techniques.definition: Organize public hearings where community members present grievances and evidence directly to government officials for accountability
techniques.example: Public hearing where MGNREGA workers present evidence of delayed wage payments to Block Development Officer, with media present
techniques.southAsianContext: Pioneered by Mazdoor Kisan Shakti Sangathan (MKSS) in Rajasthan. Now used across India for scheme accountability. Creates public pressure for service delivery improvement. Particularly effective when combined with social audit findings. Media presence amplifies accountability pressure.
implementationTips: Prepare community members with evidence and data
Ensure official participation is confirmed
Invite media for accountability
Document commitments and follow up

techniques.name: Collective Efficacy Building
techniques.definition: Build a community's shared belief that they can work together to achieve change, through shared successes and joint action
techniques.example: After successfully constructing a community water tank through collective labor, village recognizes its capacity to tackle other development challenges together
techniques.southAsianContext: Collective efficacy is the community-level analog of self-efficacy. Shramdaan (voluntary labor) traditions build collective efficacy. Successful joint infrastructure projects demonstrate community capability. Kerala's People's Plan Campaign built state-wide collective efficacy. Disaster response often catalyzes community collective efficacy.
implementationTips: Start with achievable joint projects
Celebrate collective achievements publicly
Document community-led successes
Build on existing collective action traditions

techniques.name: Inter-Community Exposure Visits
techniques.definition: Organize visits for community members to observe and learn from successful behavior change in comparable communities
techniques.example: Taking farmers from a drought-prone block in Rajasthan to visit a similar community in Gujarat that has adopted drip irrigation and watershed management
techniques.southAsianContext: Exposure visits are among the most effective BCC tools in South Asian development. 'Seeing is believing' overcomes resistance to change. SBM used massive exposure visit programs. NABARD supports farmer exposure visits. SHG federation visits for institutional learning. Most effective when visiting and host communities share similar profiles.
implementationTips: Match communities by socioeconomic and geographic profile
Prepare visitors with specific learning objectives
Facilitate structured reflection after visits
Create follow-up action plans immediately

techniques.name: Community Radio for Behavior Change
techniques.definition: Use community-owned radio stations to broadcast locally relevant behavior change content, discussions, and community voices
techniques.example: Community radio station in Jharkhand broadcasting weekly maternal health program featuring local ASHA workers, mothers, and ANMs discussing pregnancy care in Sadri language
techniques.southAsianContext: Over 300 community radio stations in India under CR policy. Nepal has extensive community radio network. Uniquely suited for BCC - local language, local voices, interactive (phone-in), reaches remote/low-literacy populations. Radio Bundelkhand, Radio Mewat, and Gurgaon ki Awaaz are examples of successful health BCC through community radio.
implementationTips: Produce content in local language and dialect
Feature community members, not just experts
Include interactive call-in segments
Coordinate with frontline workers for follow-up

techniques.name: Folk Media and Street Theatre (Nukkad Natak)
techniques.definition: Use traditional performance arts - street theatre, puppet shows, folk songs, and drama - to communicate behavior change messages in culturally resonant formats
techniques.example: Kala Jatha troupe performing a play about the importance of girls' education and the harms of child marriage in weekly market gatherings across rural MP
techniques.southAsianContext: Rich tradition of folk media for social change - Nukkad Natak, Kala Jatha, puppet shows (Kathputli in Rajasthan), Bhavai, Tamasha. Used by Jan Natya Manch, Nishant Natya Manch. Government campaigns use Kala Jathas for scheme awareness. Meena communicates initiative in South Asia used animated character for girls' education.
implementationTips: Engage local folk artists and traditions
Develop participatory scripts with community input
Include humor and entertainment value
Follow up performances with facilitated discussion
Use market days and festivals for maximum reach

caseStudies.title: Nukkad Natak for Menstrual Hygiene in Madhya Pradesh
caseStudies.description: Street theatre groups performed skits addressing menstrual hygiene taboos and promoting safe menstrual waste disposal in villages of Hoshangabad district. Performances used humor and familiar village scenarios to normalize conversations around menstruation and sanitary pad disposal.
caseStudies.outcome: Post-performance surveys showed 63% increase in willingness to discuss menstrual hygiene openly; safe menstrual waste disposal practices increased from 14% to 41% over 6 months.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from WaterSHED and WASH United menstrual hygiene management programs, Madhya Pradesh

techniques.name: Women's Collective Action
techniques.definition: Facilitate organized women's groups taking collective action to address behavior change barriers and enforce community norms
techniques.example: Women's group in Manipur collectively deciding to close all alcohol shops in their village and taking shifts to enforce the decision
techniques.southAsianContext: Women's movements like Anti-Arrack movement in AP, Meira Paibi in Manipur, and Chipko movement demonstrate women's collective power. SHG federations undertake collective action for alcohol prohibition, child marriage prevention, and domestic violence response. Mahila Mandals, Jagriti Samiti, and VHSNC sub-committees channel collective agency.
implementationTips: Support but don't direct women's agenda-setting
Build on existing women's organizations
Ensure safety and protection for activists
Connect to legal frameworks for enforcement

techniques.name: Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA)
techniques.definition: Use visual, participatory tools like social mapping, wealth ranking, seasonal calendars, and transect walks to enable communities to analyze their own situation and plan behavior changes
techniques.example: Community social mapping exercise revealing that all open defecation sites are near Dalit habitations, triggering collective action for equitable sanitation
techniques.southAsianContext: PRA methodology developed by Robert Chambers is widely used across South Asian development. Tools include social mapping, resource mapping, seasonal calendars, Venn diagrams, and transect walks. Generates community-owned data for planning. NRLM uses PRA for village poverty reduction plans. Most effective when followed by action planning.
implementationTips: Use local facilitators trained in PRA tools
Ensure women and marginalized groups participate in mapping
Follow analysis with concrete action planning
Display PRA outputs in community spaces

techniques.name: Youth-Led Campaigns
techniques.definition: Engage young people as designers and leaders of behavior change campaigns targeting their peers and communities
techniques.example: NSS volunteers leading a village-level clean-up drive followed by wall painting campaign on waste segregation messages designed by college students
techniques.southAsianContext: Nehru Yuva Kendra Sangathan, NSS, NCC, and youth clubs provide institutional platforms. Yuwaah (UNICEF) engages youth in social change. Youth-led campaigns on digital safety, substance abuse, menstrual health, and environmental action. Young people bring energy, creativity, and social media savvy. Must provide genuine leadership roles, not just token participation.
implementationTips: Give youth genuine leadership and decision-making power
Provide training in campaign design and communication
Use social media channels youth already use
Connect with existing youth organizations

techniques.name: Convergent Action Planning
techniques.definition: Bring together multiple government departments and community stakeholders to create coordinated behavior change plans that address interconnected issues
techniques.example: Block-level convergence meeting where Health, WCD, WASH, Education, and Panchayat departments create joint action plan for a malnourished pocket, aligning their BCC efforts
techniques.southAsianContext: Poshan Abhiyaan's convergence model brings together WCD, Health, WASH, Education, and Agriculture for nutrition. NITI Aayog's Aspirational Districts Programme coordinates departments. District Collector-led convergence meetings. Key challenge: departmental silos and vertical programming. When convergence works, it multiplies BCC impact by aligning messages and services.
implementationTips: Use District Collector's authority for coordination
Create shared dashboards for monitoring
Align BCC messages across departments
Identify a convergence nodal officer

techniques.name: Faith-Based Organization Partnerships
techniques.definition: Partner with temples, mosques, churches, gurdwaras, and faith-based organizations to deliver behavior change messaging through trusted religious networks
techniques.example: Partnering with All India Imam Organization to include immunization safety messages during Friday sermons across 500,000 mosques, directly addressing vaccine hesitancy in Muslim communities
techniques.southAsianContext: Faith organizations have unparalleled reach and trust in South Asian communities. Polio eradication in India succeeded partly through systematic religious engagement. Temple-based health camps. Gurdwara langars promoting nutrition awareness. Church-based health programs in Northeast India. Madrasa-based education improvements. Must be genuine partnerships respecting theological autonomy.
implementationTips: Approach with genuine respect for religious autonomy
Find alignment between health messages and religious values
Support existing religious welfare activities
Work across faiths in multi-religious communities

categories.name: Cultural & Narrative Approaches
categories.description: Techniques using storytelling, cultural practices, religious engagement, and narrative frameworks for behavior change

techniques.name: Storytelling and Narrative Persuasion (Katha)
techniques.definition: Use stories, parables, and narrative formats to convey behavior change messages in culturally resonant ways
techniques.example: Adapting the traditional Katha format to tell the story of a family that overcame malnutrition through dietary diversity, shared at community gatherings
techniques.southAsianContext: Oral storytelling (Katha, Kissa, Dastaan) is deeply embedded in South Asian culture. Kathas during religious gatherings, bedtime stories, and community narrative traditions provide natural BCC delivery channels. Meena stories (UNICEF), Kahaani (Population Foundation), and Radio Natak use narrative formats. Research shows stories change attitudes more effectively than facts alone.
implementationTips: Base stories on real community experiences
Use familiar narrative structures (Katha, folk tale)
Include relatable characters from similar backgrounds
End with hopeful, actionable resolution
Train community storytellers

caseStudies.title: Grandmother Storytelling for Infant Feeding in India
caseStudies.description: In West Bengal's Purulia district, elder women shared personal narratives about child feeding practices that worked well in their families during Nutrition Awareness sessions, reframing traditional knowledge within evidence-based complementary feeding guidance and challenging harmful practices like delaying complementary foods.
caseStudies.outcome: Timely introduction of complementary feeding at 6 months increased from 42% to 71%; the grandmother engagement approach reduced family-level resistance to recommended feeding practices by 53%.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from ICDS-World Bank ISSNIP grandmother engagement pilots, West Bengal

techniques.name: Religious Leader Engagement
techniques.definition: Partner with religious leaders and institutions to endorse and promote behavior change through religious teachings and community trust
techniques.example: Imam delivering Friday sermon on the Islamic duty to keep children healthy through immunization, citing Quranic verses on protecting life
techniques.southAsianContext: Religious leaders have enormous influence across South Asia. Polio eradication in India succeeded partly through ulema engagement. Temple committees promote health campaigns during festivals. Buddhist monks in Sri Lanka and Myanmar support nutrition programs. Must respect theological integrity while aligning health messages with religious values.
implementationTips: Approach religious leaders with respect and genuine partnership
Find alignment between health messages and religious teachings
Never instrumentalize religion - ensure genuine engagement
Work across faiths in multi-religious communities

caseStudies.title: Mosque-Based Handwashing Promotion in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: Imams in Rajshahi division integrated handwashing messages into Friday khutbahs (sermons), linking ritual ablution (wudu) practices to health-protective handwashing with soap. Mosques also installed handwashing stations with soap near entrance areas.
caseStudies.outcome: Mosque-goers' handwashing with soap at critical times increased by 41% in intervention areas; the religious framing proved more sustainable than health-messaging alone at 12-month follow-up.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from BRAC WASH programme religious leader engagement component

techniques.name: Festival-Linked Campaigns
techniques.definition: Time and theme behavior change campaigns to align with major festivals, leveraging heightened community engagement and cultural resonance
techniques.example: Launching a clean village campaign during Diwali, connecting cleanliness of homes and surroundings to the festival's emphasis on light, purity, and new beginnings
techniques.southAsianContext: South Asia's dense festival calendar provides natural campaign opportunities. Diwali for cleanliness (SBM), Raksha Bandhan for gender equity, Eid for community meals/nutrition, Pongal/Makar Sankranti for harvest/agriculture, Chhath for water conservation. Festival associations create positive emotional connections to behavior change.
implementationTips: Map local festival calendar for campaign planning
Connect behavior change to festival's core values
Use festival gatherings as distribution/demonstration points
Create festival-specific IEC materials

techniques.name: Local Proverb and Idiom Integration
techniques.definition: Embed behavior change messages within local proverbs, sayings, and idiomatic expressions that carry cultural authority
techniques.example: Using the Hindi proverb 'Pehla sukh nirogi kaya' (First happiness is a healthy body) to frame health-seeking behavior messaging
techniques.southAsianContext: South Asian languages are rich in health, agriculture, and wisdom proverbs. Embedding BCC messages in familiar sayings increases acceptance and recall. Tribal and regional languages have unique proverb traditions. Must be used authentically, not manufactured. Community members can identify the most resonant proverbs for each message.
implementationTips: Collect proverbs from community members, not external sources
Use in local language, never translate
Combine with visual representation
Integrate into IEC materials and frontline worker scripts

techniques.name: Cultural Broker Engagement
techniques.definition: Identify and engage individuals who bridge traditional and modern knowledge systems to facilitate culturally appropriate behavior change
techniques.example: A trained dai (traditional birth attendant) who has also received HBNC training serves as a bridge between traditional birth practices and institutional delivery messaging
techniques.southAsianContext: Traditional practitioners (dais, vaidyas, hakims, jhad-phunk practitioners) hold community trust. Rather than marginalizing them, training them as cultural brokers improves BCC effectiveness. ASHA herself is designed as a cultural broker - community member with health training. Farmer innovators bridge traditional and modern agriculture.
implementationTips: Respect traditional practitioners' knowledge and status
Provide complementary training, not replacement
Create formal recognition for cultural brokers
Monitor for potential misinformation

techniques.name: Life-Course Event Messaging
techniques.definition: Link behavior change communication to key life events (pregnancy, birth, marriage, harvest) when families are naturally receptive to new information
techniques.example: Delivering comprehensive nutrition counseling at the time of marriage registration, when the new family is forming habits and receptive to guidance
techniques.southAsianContext: South Asian life course events are highly ritualized and create natural counseling windows. Antenatal registration triggers health messaging. Marriage is an opportunity for family planning and nutrition counseling. Birth of first child opens window for exclusive breastfeeding support. School enrollment triggers child development messaging.
implementationTips: Map life-course events and corresponding BCC opportunities
Train service providers at each touchpoint
Develop event-specific counseling modules
Reach family decision-makers, not just the individual

caseStudies.title: Annaprashan (First Rice Feeding) Ceremony as Nutrition Entry Point in India
caseStudies.description: In Odisha's Koraput district, ICDS workers leveraged the annaprashan ceremony (infant's first solid food, typically at 6 months) as a culturally appropriate entry point for complementary feeding counseling, providing families with food preparation guidance and a locally produced complementary food starter kit.
caseStudies.outcome: Families receiving annaprashan-linked counseling initiated complementary feeding with appropriate foods 28 days earlier on average; cultural framing reduced rejection of nutrition advice by 61%.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from ICDS life-course nutrition messaging approaches, Odisha tribal districts

techniques.name: Seasonal Calendar Alignment
techniques.definition: Align behavior change interventions with agricultural and seasonal cycles that govern daily life in rural communities
techniques.example: Scheduling immunization drives during the lean season when farming families are not busy with sowing/harvesting and are available at village
techniques.southAsianContext: Rural South Asian life is governed by agricultural seasons - sowing, monsoon, harvest, lean season. Migration patterns follow seasons. Health-seeking behavior varies with seasons. Nutrition interventions must account for seasonal food availability. WASH behaviors shift between dry and monsoon seasons. Effective BCC programs align with these rhythms.
implementationTips: Map local agricultural and migration calendar
Schedule intensive BCC during lean/available seasons
Adapt messages to seasonal disease patterns
Account for seasonal food availability in nutrition messaging

caseStudies.title: Seasonal Food Calendar for Nutrition in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In Satkhira and Khulna's climate-vulnerable coastal communities, nutrition workers developed season-specific dietary guidance calendars showing which locally available, affordable, and nutritious foods were accessible during each Bengali month, with recipes and preservation techniques for lean seasons.
caseStudies.outcome: Minimum dietary diversity during lean seasons improved from 19% to 48%; the seasonal calendar approach was most effective in maintaining dietary diversity during monsoon and cyclone seasons.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from FAO-Bangladesh seasonal nutrition programming in climate-vulnerable areas

techniques.name: Indigenous Knowledge Integration
techniques.definition: Incorporate traditional and indigenous knowledge systems into behavior change programs, validating local wisdom while introducing complementary evidence-based practices
techniques.example: Integrating tribal knowledge of nutritious wild foods (tubers, leafy greens, mushrooms) into Poshan Abhiyaan dietary diversity messaging
techniques.southAsianContext: South Asia has rich indigenous knowledge in agriculture (traditional seed varieties, organic farming), health (Ayurveda, Siddha, Unani, tribal medicine), and natural resource management (sacred groves, traditional water harvesting). Programs that build on indigenous knowledge rather than replacing it achieve better adoption and sustainability.
implementationTips: Document indigenous knowledge before introducing new practices
Identify complementarities between traditional and modern approaches
Give credit to indigenous knowledge holders
Create dialogue between knowledge systems, not replacement

caseStudies.title: Traditional Food Revival for Nutrition in India
caseStudies.description: In Odisha's Rayagada and Koraput tribal districts, the program documented and promoted nutritious indigenous foods (ragi/finger millet, various uncultivated greens, tubers, small millets) that were being abandoned in favor of polished rice, integrating traditional food knowledge with modern nutritional science.
caseStudies.outcome: Consumption of traditional nutrient-dense foods increased by 38% in intervention villages; dietary diversity scores improved most in communities that reconnected with indigenous food systems; iron and zinc intake from local foods increased significantly.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from LANSA (Leveraging Agriculture for Nutrition in South Asia) research programs, Odisha

techniques.name: Edutainment and Serial Drama
techniques.definition: Use entertainment media - TV/radio serial dramas, films, and web series - to model behavior change through character development and storylines
techniques.example: Main Kuch Bhi Kar Sakti Hoon (Population Foundation of India) TV serial depicting a female doctor challenging gender norms in a small town, with associated community engagement
techniques.southAsianContext: India has strong edutainment tradition - Hum Log (India's first soap opera, 1984) addressed family planning. Main Kuch Bhi Kar Sakti Hoon reached 400+ million. Bangladesh's Meena and Sesame Street's Sisimpur. Radio serials through AIR reach rural populations. Edutainment changes norms by creating parasocial relationships with characters who model progressive behaviors.
implementationTips: Develop characters audiences identify with
Show gradual transformation, not instant change
Include community discussion guides for each episode
Use multiple platforms (TV, radio, digital, community screening)

techniques.name: Wall Art and Murals for BCC
techniques.definition: Use painted walls, murals, and public art in villages and urban spaces to communicate behavior change messages visually and continuously
techniques.example: Professionally designed wall painting at the village entrance depicting a happy family using a toilet, washing hands, and eating diverse foods, with key messages in local language
techniques.southAsianContext: Wall writing is India's most ubiquitous advertising medium and serves BCC effectively. SBM wall paintings ('Shauchalay banana zaroori hai'), immunization messages at PHCs, and anti-dowry slogans at panchayat buildings. When done artistically, walls become community landmarks. Must be maintained - faded walls undermine the message. Community-designed murals create ownership.
implementationTips: Use professional artists for visual quality
Involve community in message and design selection
Place at high-visibility locations
Maintain and refresh periodically

techniques.name: Leveraging Kinship and Caste Networks
techniques.definition: Use existing kinship, gotra, biradari, and caste-based networks as channels for behavior change communication and norm diffusion
techniques.example: Working with the jati panchayat (caste council) leadership in Rajasthan to endorse girl child education and discourage child marriage within the community
techniques.southAsianContext: South Asian societies are organized around kinship and caste networks that transmit information, norms, and sanctions. Biradari panchayats, gotra networks, and caste associations are powerful channels. Using them for progressive behavior change (education, health) can be transformative. Must navigate carefully - caste networks can also reinforce harmful hierarchies.
implementationTips: Engage caste/kinship leaders for progressive messaging
Be sensitive to reinforcing hierarchies
Use networks for information flow, not enforcement
Create cross-caste platforms alongside caste-specific engagement

categories.name: Gender Transformative Approaches
categories.description: Techniques that actively challenge and transform harmful gender norms, promote women's agency, and engage men and boys in gender equity

techniques.name: Gender Dialogue Sessions
techniques.definition: Facilitate structured conversations between men and women to examine and challenge gender roles, power dynamics, and norms that affect behavior
techniques.example: Couples dialogue sessions in UP where husbands and wives discuss household decision-making, leading to shared decisions on family planning and child nutrition
techniques.southAsianContext: Programs like GEMS (Gender Equity Movement in Schools), Breakthrough's Bell Bajao, and Oxfam's We Can campaign use structured gender dialogues. Effective in shifting attitudes on domestic violence, women's mobility, and reproductive autonomy. Must create safe spaces and have skilled facilitators to manage power dynamics.
implementationTips: Use trained facilitators who understand local gender dynamics
Create separate and joint gender sessions
Include practical exercises, not just discussion
Follow up with ongoing support groups

techniques.name: Engaging Men and Boys
techniques.definition: Design specific interventions targeting men and boys to examine masculinity norms and become allies in gender equity and behavior change
techniques.example: Yaari Dosti program engaging young men in reflecting on masculinity norms and supporting partners' reproductive health choices
techniques.southAsianContext: Programs like ICRW's Yaari Dosti, Population Council's Do Kadam, and Save the Children's Choices curriculum work with men and boys. Male engagement is critical because men often control household decisions on health spending, women's mobility, and family planning. Must challenge without alienating.
implementationTips: Use male peer facilitators
Create safe spaces for men to reflect without judgment
Build on positive aspects of masculinity
Connect to fatherhood and family wellbeing

techniques.name: Addressing Son Preference and Sex Selection
techniques.definition: Challenge cultural norms favoring sons through awareness, positive valuation of daughters, and enforcement of PCPNDT Act
techniques.example: Beti Bachao Beti Padhao campaign celebrating girls' achievements and creating 'daughter festivals' in communities with adverse sex ratios
techniques.southAsianContext: Adverse sex ratio persists in parts of India (Haryana, Punjab, UP, Rajasthan), Nepal, and Bangladesh. BBBP uses multi-pronged approach: enforcement, awareness, and celebration. Community celebrations of girl child birth, naming ceremonies, and achievement recognition. Must address dowry system as root cause alongside preference.
implementationTips: Combine legal enforcement with attitude change
Celebrate daughters publicly and visibly
Address dowry as underlying economic driver
Engage religious leaders against sex selection

techniques.name: Menstrual Health Normalization
techniques.definition: Challenge taboos and stigma around menstruation through education, product access, and open conversation to support girls' and women's dignity and participation
techniques.example: School-based MHM program providing pad vending machines, incinerators, and menstrual health education, reducing school absenteeism during periods
techniques.southAsianContext: Menstrual taboos restrict women's mobility, diet, religious participation, and school attendance across South Asia. Pad availability improved through government distribution and social enterprises (Arunachalam Muruganantham/Pad Man story). School WASH with MHM facilities under Swachh Vidyalaya. Breaking silence through open discussions, campaigns like #PadManChallenge.
implementationTips: Address both product access and stigma simultaneously
Engage boys and men in normalization conversations
Ensure school WASH facilities include MHM provisions
Use women's groups for peer education

caseStudies.title: School-Based Menstrual Hygiene Management in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In Achham and Doti districts where chhaupadi (menstrual exile) practices persist, school-based programs combined menstrual health education, pad-making workshops, WASH-friendly toilet upgrades, and peer discussion groups to normalize menstruation and challenge stigma.
caseStudies.outcome: Self-reported chhaupadi practice declined by 48% among schoolgoing girls; school absenteeism during menstruation reduced from 4.2 to 1.1 days per month; 72% of trained girls shared menstrual health knowledge with family members.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Rato Baltin campaign and UNICEF Nepal menstrual hygiene programs

techniques.name: Women's Economic Empowerment as Behavior Change Lever
techniques.definition: Build women's economic agency through skill building, market access, and financial control to enable broader behavior changes in health, education, and nutrition
techniques.example: When women in Bihar SHGs gained independent income through mushroom cultivation, they also gained voice in household decisions on child nutrition and healthcare spending
techniques.southAsianContext: Economic empowerment shifts intra-household power dynamics. JEEViKA, Kudumbashree, and SRLM programs show women with independent income invest more in children's health and education. Financial inclusion through Jan Dhan enables direct benefit transfers to women. Must monitor for backlash (domestic violence can increase during power transition).
implementationTips: Link economic programs with gender awareness training
Monitor for domestic violence as power shifts
Support women's financial literacy and decision-making
Create women-controlled savings and credit access

techniques.name: Safe Space Creation for Adolescent Girls
techniques.definition: Establish dedicated physical and programmatic spaces where adolescent girls can learn, discuss, and build agency away from restrictive social surveillance
techniques.example: Kishori group meeting weekly at AWC where adolescent girls discuss reproductive health, nutrition, rights, and life skills in a safe, girls-only environment
techniques.southAsianContext: SABLA/Kishori Shakti programs create safe spaces at AWCs. BRAC's ELA (Empowerment and Livelihood for Adolescents) in Bangladesh. Room to Read's girls' education programs. Population Council's safe space model. These spaces are critical in contexts where girls' mobility and peer interaction is restricted. Must negotiate with families and communities for access.
implementationTips: Negotiate with families for girls' participation
Provide practical incentives (skills, supplies)
Include life skills beyond health (financial, digital literacy)
Build peer networks that extend beyond program

techniques.name: Addressing Dowry and Child Marriage Norms
techniques.definition: Challenge economic and social norms driving dowry and early marriage through community dialogue, legal awareness, and alternative ceremonies
techniques.example: Community pledge against dowry at Gram Sabha, with families publicly committing to simple ceremonies and no dowry demand, with panchayat backing
techniques.southAsianContext: Dowry and child marriage are interconnected - families marry daughters early to reduce dowry burden. Programs like Girls Not Brides, Breakthrough, and UNFPA address through community engagement, legal awareness, and economic alternatives. PCMA awareness, village-level child protection committees, and girls' education are key strategies. Some success through 'community-declared child marriage-free zones'.
implementationTips: Address economic root causes alongside norm change
Engage community leaders and panchayat
Create alternative ceremonies/celebrations
Ensure legal awareness of PCMA and Dowry Act
Support girls' education and economic alternatives

techniques.name: Shared Household Labor Negotiation
techniques.definition: Facilitate conversations and agreements between household members to redistribute unpaid domestic and care work more equitably
techniques.example: SHG-facilitated exercise where couples create a 24-hour time diary revealing unequal work distribution and negotiate specific task-sharing commitments
techniques.southAsianContext: Women in South Asia spend 5-10x more time on unpaid care work than men. Time poverty prevents women from accessing health services, economic opportunities, and rest. Programs like Oxfam's Unpaid Care Work initiative and WE-Care use time diaries and negotiation tools. Must address deeply ingrained cultural norms about gendered work.
implementationTips: Use time diary exercises to make inequality visible
Facilitate negotiation in couples, not just awareness
Celebrate men who share domestic work
Address workplace policies alongside household norms

techniques.name: Masculinity Reflection Circles
techniques.definition: Create structured spaces for men to reflect on how rigid masculinity norms affect their own health, relationships, and wellbeing
techniques.example: Young men's group in UP discussing how 'being a man' prevents them from expressing emotions, seeking health care, or sharing housework, and making personal commitments to change
techniques.southAsianContext: Programs like ICRW's GEMS, Promundo's Program H, and CHSJ's Aajeevika program create reflective spaces for men. Toxic masculinity norms contribute to men's own poor health-seeking, substance use, and violence. Engaging men in reflective rather than accusatory conversations creates allies. Sports, entertainment, and peer groups are effective entry points.
implementationTips: Use male peer facilitators
Create non-judgmental, confidential spaces
Use relatable entry points (fatherhood, relationships)
Move from reflection to concrete behavior commitments

techniques.name: GBV Response and Prevention through BCC
techniques.definition: Address gender-based violence through community awareness, survivor support, bystander intervention training, and norm change
techniques.example: Bell Bajao campaign encouraging bystanders to intervene when they hear domestic violence by ringing the doorbell, normalizing community intervention against DV
techniques.southAsianContext: India's Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act provides legal framework. One-Stop Centres (Sakhi) and Women Helpline (181) provide services. BCC campaigns include Bell Bajao (Breakthrough), UNFPA's prevention programs, and Population Foundation's media campaigns. Must address both prevention (norm change) and response (services). Challenge: normalizing help-seeking in societies where DV is accepted.
implementationTips: Combine norm change with service awareness
Train bystanders in safe intervention
Engage men as allies, not just targets
Ensure survivor support services are available

techniques.name: Addressing GBV Through Community Norms
techniques.definition: Transform community tolerance of gender-based violence through collective norm-setting, survivor support, and accountable community systems
techniques.example: Village-level declaration of 'zero tolerance for domestic violence' with community response mechanism where neighbors and panchayat members intervene when violence is reported
techniques.southAsianContext: NFHS-5 shows 30% of women aged 18-49 have experienced physical violence. Community acceptance of DV remains high. Programs like RANI (Reducing Abuse and Neglect in India) and MASVAW (Men Against Violence and Abuse) work at community level. Protection Officers under PWDVA need community support. Community-based responses complement legal mechanisms.
implementationTips: Create community response protocols
Train bystanders in safe intervention
Ensure survivor support services are accessible
Engage men and community leaders as allies

categories.name: Digital & Technology-Enabled
categories.description: Techniques using mobile phones, digital platforms, and technology to deliver, reinforce, and scale behavior change interventions

techniques.name: IVR-Based Voice Reminders
techniques.definition: Use Interactive Voice Response systems to deliver automated voice-based behavior change messages and reminders in local languages to basic mobile phones
techniques.example: Kilkari program delivering weekly pregnancy-stage-appropriate voice messages to registered pregnant women and new mothers in their chosen language
techniques.southAsianContext: Kilkari (BBC Media Action/MoHFW) reached 10+ million women with automated IVR messages. mMitra in Mumbai provides similar service. Works with basic phones (no smartphone needed) and in local languages. Addresses literacy barriers. Registration through ASHA or AWW increases reach. Cost-effective at scale.
implementationTips: Register through trusted frontline workers
Deliver in local language and dialect
Keep messages under 2 minutes
Time delivery to avoid disruption
Include opt-out options

techniques.name: SMS and WhatsApp Nudges
techniques.definition: Send targeted text or WhatsApp messages to prompt, remind, and reinforce behavior change at optimal timing
techniques.example: Automated WhatsApp messages reminding mothers of upcoming immunization dates with the specific vaccine name and nearest health facility details
techniques.southAsianContext: WhatsApp penetration in South Asia is very high even in semi-urban and rural areas. WhatsApp groups for SHGs, farmer groups, and health workers enable peer messaging. Government schemes like PM-KISAN send SMS confirmations. TB treatment adherence uses SMS reminders (99DOTS). Must manage misinformation risks in WhatsApp groups.
implementationTips: Use WhatsApp for reach, SMS for basic phone users
Time messages to decision-making moments
Keep messages short and actionable
Monitor groups for misinformation
Respect privacy and data protection

techniques.name: Video-Based Modeling via Mobile
techniques.definition: Use short videos on smartphones showing peers performing desired behaviors, shared through WhatsApp or community viewing
techniques.example: Short video showing a farmer from a similar region demonstrating System of Rice Intensification (SRI) technique, shared in agricultural WhatsApp groups
techniques.southAsianContext: Digital Green pioneered video-based agricultural extension with community screenings. Video Volunteers creates local video content for social change. Short-form video (1-3 minutes) shared via WhatsApp is increasingly popular. Community video viewing at SHG meetings combines digital and group-based approaches. Local language and local faces increase credibility.
implementationTips: Feature local people, not actors
Keep videos under 3 minutes
Show complete behavior performance
Facilitate group discussion after viewing
Create shareable format for peer distribution

techniques.name: mHealth Applications for Frontline Workers
techniques.definition: Equip frontline health workers with mobile applications that guide counseling, track behaviors, and provide decision support at the point of care
techniques.example: ASHA using CAS (Common Application Software) on tablet to register pregnancies, provide stage-appropriate counseling, and track immunization schedules
techniques.southAsianContext: India's CAS for ASHAs, RCH portal, Poshan Tracker, and mSakhi app digitize frontline work. Bangladesh uses similar mHealth tools for community health workers. Improves counseling quality, data collection, and accountability. Challenges include device management, connectivity, and digital literacy of frontline workers.
implementationTips: Design for offline functionality
Simplify interfaces for low digital literacy
Provide ongoing technical support
Use data for supportive supervision, not punitive monitoring

caseStudies.title: ICT-Enabled Nutrition Counseling by AWWs in India
caseStudies.description: In Madhya Pradesh under the ICDS-CAS initiative, Anganwadi Workers used smartphones with pre-loaded nutrition counseling videos and growth tracking apps to deliver standardized first 1000 days messages during home visits, with automated reminders for follow-up based on each child's age and nutritional status.
caseStudies.outcome: AWWs using mHealth tools delivered 82% of recommended counseling messages versus 47% with paper-based tools; mothers visited by mHealth-equipped AWWs demonstrated 56% better recall of key nutrition behaviors.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from ICDS-CAS technology integration evaluations, Madhya Pradesh

techniques.name: Digital Payment Incentives
techniques.definition: Use digital payment systems (UPI, DBT) to deliver instant financial incentives for behavior performance, leveraging speed and transparency
techniques.example: Instant UPI transfer to ASHA upon verification of home visit completion, replacing delayed cash incentive payments
techniques.southAsianContext: Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile (JAM) trinity enables direct benefit transfers. UPI revolution makes instant micro-payments possible. Reduces leakage compared to cash incentives. PM-KISAN, JSY, and LPG subsidy use DBT. Can be linked to real-time behavior verification. Digital payments also build financial inclusion as a co-benefit.
implementationTips: Ensure beneficiaries have functional bank accounts
Provide transparent tracking of payment status
Address connectivity issues for real-time verification
Build digital literacy alongside payment systems

techniques.name: Community WhatsApp for Social Accountability
techniques.definition: Create and moderate community WhatsApp groups where citizens can report service delivery issues, share evidence, and demand accountability from duty bearers
techniques.example: Village WhatsApp group where residents post photos of broken hand pumps, absent teachers, or empty medicine shelves, with block officials added to the group
techniques.southAsianContext: Citizen-led digital accountability is growing rapidly. MyGov platform, CM Helplines, and social media-based grievance redressal supplement traditional mechanisms. WhatsApp groups with officials create real-time accountability loops. Must manage misinformation risks and ensure marginalized voices are included (digital divide).
implementationTips: Add duty bearers to community groups
Establish group norms against misinformation
Ensure marginalized community members have access
Follow up on issues raised to maintain group credibility

techniques.name: Telemedicine-Linked Behavior Support
techniques.definition: Use telemedicine consultations to provide personalized behavior change counseling and follow-up from specialist providers in remote areas
techniques.example: Diabetic patient in rural Rajasthan receiving dietary counseling and medication adherence support via video consultation with an endocrinologist in Jaipur
techniques.southAsianContext: eSanjeevani (national telemedicine platform) expanded massively during COVID-19 and continues. AMRIT (Affordable Medicines and Reliable Implants for Treatment) provides tele-counseling. Connects rural populations with specialist behavior change support. Particularly valuable for NCD management, mental health, and TB adherence. Requires connectivity infrastructure.
implementationTips: Establish connectivity at health sub-centres
Train local health workers to facilitate teleconsultations
Follow up on teleconsultation advice through home visits
Ensure patient privacy in shared consultation spaces

techniques.name: AI Chatbot Counseling
techniques.definition: Use AI-powered chatbots to provide personalized, accessible health and behavior change counseling through messaging platforms
