techniques.example: WhatsApp-based chatbot in Hindi providing personalized infant feeding advice based on child's age, answering common questions about complementary feeding
techniques.southAsianContext: AI chatbots expanding rapidly - government and private sector deploying on WhatsApp, Telegram, and custom apps. eSanjeevani OPD includes AI triage. Chatbots can address health worker shortages by providing 24/7 basic counseling. Must be designed for low-literacy users, multiple languages, and voice-based interaction. Cannot replace human relationships for complex behavior change.
implementationTips: Design for voice and text input
Support multiple South Asian languages
Integrate with human referral for complex cases
Regularly update with evidence-based content

techniques.name: Digital Health Records as Feedback
techniques.definition: Use digitized health records to provide patients and families with visual feedback on health trajectories and behavior adherence
techniques.example: Mother viewing her child's growth chart on Poshan Tracker app on ASHA's phone, seeing the trajectory over time and understanding which feeding changes helped
techniques.southAsianContext: Poshan Tracker, RCH portal, NIKSHAY (TB), and ABHA health ID are creating digital health ecosystems. When shared with beneficiaries, digital records become powerful feedback tools. Growth monitoring data on Poshan Tracker, immunization records on eVIN, and BP/sugar tracking for NCD patients all provide visual feedback. Privacy and data protection must be ensured.
implementationTips: Show beneficiaries their own data in visual format
Train frontline workers to interpret and explain records
Ensure data privacy and consent
Use trends over time for motivational feedback

techniques.name: Voice Message Campaigns in Local Languages
techniques.definition: Deliver behavior change messages through pre-recorded voice messages in local languages and dialects to overcome literacy barriers
techniques.example: Weekly voice messages in Bhojpuri delivered to registered pregnant women's phones covering trimester-specific ANC advice, narrated by a local ASHA with familiar accent and expressions
techniques.southAsianContext: India has 22 scheduled languages and hundreds of dialects. Text-based BCC excludes large populations. Voice messages through IVR, WhatsApp voice notes, and missed call services reach low-literacy populations. BBC Media Action's Mobile Kunji and Kilkari demonstrated impact at scale. Local accent and familiar narration increase trust and engagement.
implementationTips: Record in local dialect, not standard language
Use community voices, not professional narrators
Keep messages under 90 seconds
Time delivery for when phones are accessible

categories.name: Nudge & Choice Architecture
categories.description: Techniques from behavioral economics that structure choices, defaults, and decision environments to make desired behaviors easier and more likely

techniques.name: Default Option Setting
techniques.definition: Set the desired behavior as the default choice that requires no action, while making the undesired behavior require active opt-out
techniques.example: Enrolling all newborns in the immunization tracking system by default at birth registration, with reminders sent automatically
techniques.southAsianContext: Organ donation opt-out policies being explored. Automatic enrollment in Ayushman Bharat through SECC data. Default opt-in for government savings schemes. Aadhaar-linked automatic service enrollment. Default enrollment dramatically increases participation compared to opt-in. Must ensure transparency and easy opt-out.
implementationTips: Clearly communicate the default and opt-out process
Ensure transparency in default settings
Design easy opt-out for those who choose differently
Use administrative data for automatic enrollment

techniques.name: Simplification of Complex Choices
techniques.definition: Reduce the cognitive complexity of decision-making by simplifying options, forms, processes, and information
techniques.example: Redesigning the MGNREGA job card application from a multi-page form to a simple one-page format with pictorial guidance
techniques.southAsianContext: Bureaucratic complexity is the biggest barrier to scheme access in South Asia. CSC (Common Service Centres) simplify digital applications. Jan Dhan's simplified KYC opened millions of bank accounts. Ration card simplification improved PDS access. Health insurance claim simplification under PMJAY. Every simplification step increases adoption.
implementationTips: Test forms and processes with lowest-literacy users
Use pictorial guidance and local language
Reduce documentation requirements
Provide facilitation support at access points

techniques.name: Commitment Devices
techniques.definition: Create mechanisms that bind a person to their future intentions, making it costly or difficult to deviate from planned behavior
techniques.example: SHG savings lock-in period where members commit their savings for 6 months and cannot withdraw without group approval, helping them overcome temptation to spend
techniques.southAsianContext: SHG savings rules function as commitment devices. CICO (commitment savings) products in microfinance. Health camp pre-registration as commitment. Crop insurance enrollment before sowing season. Public pledges at Gram Sabha. Commitment devices work well when they align with existing social structures and community accountability.
implementationTips: Design flexible commitment with community accountability
Allow emergency exit provisions
Use social (not just financial) commitment
Leverage existing group structures for enforcement

caseStudies.title: Household Sanitation Commitment Locks in India
caseStudies.description: In Gujarat's Banaskantha district, households publicly committed to toilet use by accepting 'commitment padlocks' placed on their defunct open defecation paths, symbolizing their pledge. Progress was tracked through community monitoring visits.
caseStudies.outcome: Villages using commitment devices achieved ODF status 2 months faster than control villages; 77% of committed households maintained exclusive toilet use at 12-month follow-up.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from FINISH Society toilet-use sustenance interventions, Gujarat

techniques.name: Friction Reduction
techniques.definition: Remove small barriers, delays, and hassles that disproportionately prevent people from performing desired behaviors
techniques.example: Bringing immunization services to the village through outreach sessions rather than requiring mothers to travel to the PHC, eliminating transport friction
techniques.southAsianContext: Service delivery friction is a major behavior barrier - distance to facility, waiting time, documentation requirements, operating hours. Outreach services (VHND, immunization camps, health melas) reduce friction. Mobile banking agents bring services to doorstep. Anytime service (24x7 PHCs) reduces timing friction.
implementationTips: Map the journey from intention to behavior completion
Identify and remove each friction point
Bring services to people, not people to services
Reduce wait times and documentation requirements

techniques.name: Social Proof Displays
techniques.definition: Make the prevalence of desired behavior visible in the physical environment to establish it as the social norm
techniques.example: Displaying a large board at village entrance showing '87 out of 100 families in this village use toilets' with household names listed
techniques.southAsianContext: SBM used village-level ODF declaration boards. Immunization coverage displays at AWCs. 'Green rating' stickers on homes that practice waste segregation. School attendance charts displayed publicly. Social proof is powerful in communities where social conformity is valued. Must be accurate - false social proof undermines trust.
implementationTips: Ensure displayed data is accurate and updated
Use visual formats accessible to low-literacy populations
Display at high-visibility community points
Update regularly to show progress

caseStudies.title: Clean Village Status Displays for WASH in India
caseStudies.description: In Uttarakhand's Tehri Garhwal district, villages displayed prominently updated boards showing the percentage of households practicing safe water storage, handwashing with soap, and toilet use, creating visible social proof of community norms shifting.
caseStudies.outcome: Villages with social proof displays showed 29% higher adoption of complete WASH behavior packages; the displays were most effective in smaller, more cohesive communities.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Swachh Bharat Mission social and behavior change communication strategies, Uttarakhand

techniques.name: Anchoring and Reference Points
techniques.definition: Provide a reference number or standard that influences subsequent judgments and decisions about behavior
techniques.example: Telling farmers that the recommended fertilizer application is 120 kg/hectare, anchoring their decision around this reference rather than over-applying
techniques.southAsianContext: Government recommended doses for fertilizer, seed rate, and pesticide application serve as anchors. Nutrition guidelines (dietary diversity score of 4+) anchor feeding practices. Savings targets in SHGs anchor savings behavior. Health worker performance standards anchor effort. Anchors must be realistic and achievable for the population.
implementationTips: Set achievable reference points
Make the anchor locally relevant
Communicate through trusted channels
Adjust anchors based on baseline behavior

caseStudies.title: Portion Size Anchoring for Child Feeding in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In Dhading district, nutrition counselors used locally familiar objects as portion size anchors - a child's fist for rice/grain quantity, an egg-sized portion for protein, a small banana leaf cup for dal - making abstract complementary feeding quantity recommendations concrete and culturally intuitive.
caseStudies.outcome: Mothers using anchored portion guidance provided 42% larger and more nutritionally adequate complementary food portions; the approach was particularly effective for first-time mothers who lacked feeding experience.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Helen Keller International complementary feeding programs, Nepal

techniques.name: Framing Effects
techniques.definition: Present the same information in different frames (gain vs. loss, frequency vs. percentage) to influence how it is perceived and acted upon
techniques.example: Framing immunization as '9 out of 10 children in your village are protected' (gain frame) rather than '1 in 10 children is still at risk' (loss frame)
techniques.southAsianContext: Gain framing works better for preventive health behaviors, loss framing for detection/treatment behaviors. Nutrition messaging framed as 'your child will be smarter' (gain) vs. 'your child may be stunted' (loss). Agricultural advice framed as yield increase (gain) vs. yield loss prevention (loss). Must test frames with local audiences.
implementationTips: Test different frames with target audience
Use gain frames for preventive behaviors
Use loss frames for detection/screening behaviors
Frame in terms familiar to the audience

techniques.name: Temptation Bundling
techniques.definition: Pair an unpleasant or tedious behavior with an enjoyable activity to increase the likelihood of performing the desired behavior
techniques.example: Playing Bollywood music and creating a festive atmosphere at immunization camps to make the experience enjoyable for families
techniques.southAsianContext: Health camps with entertainment and social gathering components increase attendance. Mid-day meal combines nutrition with education attendance. SHG meetings that include socializing, skill-building, and savings. Community events combining health screening with cultural programs. The 'mela' (fair) model naturally bundles health services with entertainment.
implementationTips: Identify what the target audience enjoys
Create natural pairings, not forced combinations
Use the 'mela' or 'camp' model for service delivery
Include social and entertainment elements in health programs

techniques.name: Loss Aversion Leveraging
techniques.definition: Frame behavior change in terms of what people stand to lose by not acting, leveraging the psychological tendency to weigh losses more heavily than equivalent gains
techniques.example: Informing PM-KISAN eligible farmers that they will lose Rs. 6000 annual benefit if they don't update their Aadhaar linkage by the deadline
techniques.southAsianContext: Loss framing is powerful for scheme enrollment deadlines, insurance renewal, and subsidy continuation. PMJAY coverage 'expiry' framing encourages renewal. Crop insurance enrollment deadline framing. SHG loan eligibility loss for non-attendance. Must be used honestly - false urgency erodes trust.
implementationTips: Use real deadlines and consequences, not false urgency
Clearly communicate what will be lost
Provide easy action steps to prevent loss
Follow up with those who haven't acted near deadline

techniques.name: Micro-Incentives
techniques.definition: Provide very small but immediate rewards for specific behaviors, leveraging the disproportionate motivational impact of small certain rewards over larger uncertain ones
techniques.example: Providing a small packet of nutritious chikki (energy bar) to every child who comes for immunization, creating positive association with health facility visits
techniques.southAsianContext: Small, immediate incentives are more effective than large delayed ones. Stickers for children at immunization. Tea/snacks at community meetings. Small token gifts at enrollment drives. ASHA incentives for each home visit. Key principle: the reward must be immediate, certain, and tangible. Even symbolic micro-incentives (stamps, stickers) work for children.
implementationTips: Deliver immediately upon behavior completion
Even symbolic rewards (stickers, stamps) are effective
Keep cost minimal but experience positive
Pair with social recognition

techniques.name: Pre-Commitment Strategies
techniques.definition: Ask people to commit to a future behavior at a time when their motivation is high and barriers seem manageable
techniques.example: During pregnancy registration, asking mothers to pre-commit to exclusive breastfeeding and institutional delivery while motivation is high
techniques.southAsianContext: Pre-commitment during ANC visits for institutional delivery and breastfeeding. Pre-season crop insurance enrollment when harvest memory is fresh. Pre-commitment to savings targets at SHG annual planning. Pre-commitment works because people overestimate their future willpower. The commitment creates a reference point they feel accountable to.
implementationTips: Capture commitment during high-motivation moments
Make commitment specific and concrete
Create reminders of the commitment near the behavior
Combine with social commitment (group witnessing)

techniques.name: Implementation Intentions (If-Then Plans)
techniques.definition: Help people form specific if-then plans linking situational cues to behavioral responses, automating decision-making
techniques.example: Helping a mother form the plan: 'IF my baby cries after 2 hours of last feed, THEN I will breastfeed before offering any other food'
techniques.southAsianContext: Implementation intentions are highly effective for health behaviors that compete with ingrained habits. Applied to handwashing (IF I finish using the toilet, THEN I wash with soap), feeding (IF baby turns 6 months, THEN I start giving mashed dal-rice), and health seeking (IF child has fever for 2 days, THEN I go to PHC). Simple to teach and implement.
implementationTips: Keep if-then plans very simple and specific
Practice verbalizing the plan multiple times
Choose cues that reliably occur
Write or draw the plan for visual reminder

techniques.name: Choice Overload Reduction
techniques.definition: Reduce the number of options presented to people to prevent decision paralysis and increase the likelihood of action
techniques.example: Instead of presenting all government schemes at once, SHG facilitator presents only the 3 most relevant schemes based on the family's profile and immediate needs
techniques.southAsianContext: India's 700+ government schemes create massive choice overload for beneficiaries. Personalized scheme recommendations based on household profiles reduce cognitive burden. CSCs and e-Mitra centers help navigate options. Curated crop advisory from KVKs reduces farmer decision fatigue. Choice reduction is especially important for low-literacy populations.
implementationTips: Curate options based on user profile
Present 3-5 options maximum
Use decision trees for guided choice
Provide personalized recommendations through frontline workers

techniques.name: Sunk Cost Highlighting
techniques.definition: Remind people of investments already made in a behavior change process to motivate continuation rather than abandonment
techniques.example: Reminding a mother who completed 3 out of 4 ANC visits that she has invested significantly in her baby's health and the final visit completes the process
techniques.southAsianContext: Program dropouts often occur when the goal is partially achieved. Immunization programs remind families of doses already completed. Toilet construction follow-up reminds households of money already invested. SHG savings reminders highlight accumulated corpus. Course completion reminders on educational platforms. Works because people hate 'wasting' prior investments.
implementationTips: Quantify and communicate prior investment clearly
Show how close they are to completion
Make the remaining effort seem small relative to investment
Use visual completion trackers

techniques.name: Salience and Attention Capture
techniques.definition: Make the desired behavior or its benefits highly noticeable and attention-grabbing in the decision environment through visual design, color, size, or novelty
techniques.example: Painting the path to the handwashing station in bright colors and adding fun footprint stickers that children want to follow, making the handwashing station impossible to miss
techniques.southAsianContext: Color-coded ration cards (BPL, AAY, APL) create visual salience for entitlements. Traffic-light growth charts (red/yellow/green) make nutritional status salient. Color-coded waste bins. Bright wall paintings at health facilities. Rangoli patterns leading to health messages. Must compete with commercial advertising for attention in increasingly media-saturated environments.
implementationTips: Use bright colors and visual contrast
Place salient cues at decision points
Design for children where appropriate (play, fun)
Refresh designs to maintain novelty

categories.name: Health-Specific BCC
categories.description: Sector-specific behavior change techniques for health, nutrition, WASH, and disease control programs in South Asia

techniques.name: WASH Behavior Change Package
techniques.definition: Integrated approach combining handwashing promotion, safe water treatment, sanitation use, and menstrual hygiene through multi-channel BCC
techniques.example: School WASH program combining tippy-tap installation, handwashing songs, peer hygiene monitors, and daily practice sessions under Swachh Vidyalaya
techniques.southAsianContext: India's SBM, Jal Jeevan Mission, and Swachh Vidyalaya integrate WASH behaviors. Bangladesh's WASH Alliance uses community approaches. Key behaviors: handwashing at 5 critical times, safe water storage, toilet use, MHM. WASH behavior change requires sustained effort - initial adoption without habituation leads to 'slippage' (reverting to old behavior).
implementationTips: Focus on habit formation, not just initial adoption
Monitor for slippage over time
Integrate hardware (infrastructure) with software (BCC)
Use school-based programs to reach households through children

caseStudies.title: Integrated WASH BCC in Urban Slums of Dhaka
caseStudies.description: WaterAid's integrated WASH BCC package in Dhaka's Korail and Mirpur slums combined hardware (shared toilet maintenance systems, water point upgrades) with software (hygiene promotion sessions, caretaker training, landlord engagement) targeting handwashing, safe water chain, and sanitation.
caseStudies.outcome: Diarrheal disease incidence reduced by 36% in intervention slums; the integrated package was more effective than standalone hygiene promotion, with hardware-software combination showing synergistic effects.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from WaterAid Bangladesh urban WASH program evaluations, Dhaka

caseStudies.title: Comprehensive WASH Package in Nepal Hill Communities
caseStudies.description: In Sindhupalchok district post-earthquake, an integrated WASH package combined water system rehabilitation, school and health facility WASH improvements, and community-level behavior change using trained female community health volunteers conducting household visits with WASH kits.
caseStudies.outcome: Safe water handling practices improved from 28% to 74%; handwashing with soap at critical moments increased from 15% to 58% within 18 months of the integrated intervention.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from post-earthquake WASH recovery programs, Sindhupalchok district

techniques.name: Immunization Promotion and Demand Generation
techniques.definition: Multi-pronged approach to increase vaccine uptake through awareness, trust-building, reminder systems, and addressing vaccine hesitancy
techniques.example: Mission Indradhanush intensified drive combining mobilization by ASHAs, mosque announcements by Imams, due-list tracking, and session-site social celebrations
techniques.southAsianContext: India's UIP is the world's largest immunization program. Mission Indradhanush targets unreached children. Key strategies: due-list based tracking, ASHA mobilization, religious leader engagement (critical in Muslim communities), outreach to migrant populations, and addressing hesitancy through trusted intermediaries. Polio eradication's success provides a model.
implementationTips: Use due-list tracking for personalized follow-up
Engage religious leaders for hesitant communities
Bring services to hard-to-reach populations
Celebrate full immunization completion
Address specific concerns, not general awareness

techniques.name: First 1000 Days Nutrition Counseling
techniques.definition: Stage-specific nutrition behavior change from conception through the child's second birthday, targeting feeding, dietary diversity, and care practices
techniques.example: AWW providing trimester-specific counseling to pregnant women on iron-rich foods, then breastfeeding counseling at delivery, then complementary feeding guidance from 6 months
techniques.southAsianContext: Poshan Abhiyaan's core strategy targets the first 1000 days window. ICDS provides the platform. Key behaviors: adequate diet during pregnancy, early and exclusive breastfeeding, timely and diverse complementary feeding, full immunization, and growth monitoring. India's stunting rates (35%) show the scale of the challenge. Multi-generational messaging reaches mothers-in-law too.
implementationTips: Deliver stage-specific messages at each contact point
Include mothers-in-law in counseling
Use recipe demonstrations with local foods
Track through growth monitoring at VHND

caseStudies.title: Integrated First 1000 Days Counseling in India
caseStudies.description: In Uttar Pradesh's Bahraich district, a layered counseling approach addressed each stage of the first 1000 days: pregnancy nutrition and IFA compliance, early initiation and exclusive breastfeeding, timely complementary feeding, and dietary diversity. Home visits used stage-specific pictorial counseling cards.
caseStudies.outcome: Exclusive breastfeeding rates increased from 41% to 68%; adequate dietary diversity at 9-11 months improved from 16% to 45%; the staged approach was more effective than one-time counseling.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from UNICEF-supported IYCF counseling programs, Uttar Pradesh

caseStudies.title: Community-Based 1000 Days Nutrition Program in Sri Lanka
caseStudies.description: In Mullaitivu district, post-conflict nutrition recovery combined growth monitoring with intensive first 1000 days counseling through Public Health Midwife home visits, community Thriposha distribution, and mothers' group peer support focused on dietary diversity and responsive feeding.
caseStudies.outcome: Stunting prevalence in the target population decreased from 29% to 17% over 3 years; the integrated community-facility approach was credited with sustaining behavior change through repeated reinforcement.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from WFP and MoH Sri Lanka post-conflict nutrition recovery programs, Northern Province

techniques.name: TB Treatment Adherence Support
techniques.definition: Behavioral support systems ensuring TB patients complete their full treatment course, combining monitoring, incentives, and peer support
techniques.example: TB patient receiving daily 99DOTS medication reminders, monthly nutritional support kit, and peer counselor home visits throughout the 6-month treatment
techniques.southAsianContext: India has the world's highest TB burden. NIKSHAY Poshan Yojana provides nutritional support. 99DOTS uses technology for adherence monitoring. DOTS (Directly Observed Therapy) remains the backbone. Community-based treatment supporters reduce default. Stigma is a major barrier - confidential support systems are essential. MDR-TB requires 18+ months of adherence.
implementationTips: Combine technology with human support
Address nutritional needs alongside medication
Ensure confidentiality to reduce stigma
Provide treatment supporters from community
Use incentives for completion, not just initiation

techniques.name: Family Planning Counseling (Informed Choice)
techniques.definition: Provide comprehensive, non-coercive counseling on the full range of contraceptive methods, supporting autonomous and informed decision-making
techniques.example: ANM at sub-centre presenting the 'cafeteria approach' - showing all available methods, explaining benefits and side-effects of each, and supporting the couple's choice without pushing any particular method
techniques.southAsianContext: India's family planning program evolved from coercive (Emergency era sterilization) to rights-based informed choice. Method mix historically skewed toward female sterilization. Expanding access to reversible methods (IUCDs, injectables, pills, condoms). Male participation in family planning remains low. Spacing methods promoted through Mission Parivar Vikas. Adolescent SRH counseling gaining acceptance.
implementationTips: Never set method-specific targets
Counsel couples together where possible
Present full method mix including male methods
Address myths about specific methods
Respect reproductive autonomy completely

techniques.name: ORS-Zinc Promotion for Diarrhea Management
techniques.definition: Promote home-based oral rehydration and zinc supplementation as first-line treatment for childhood diarrhea, replacing inappropriate antibiotic use
techniques.example: Training mothers at AWC to prepare ORS solution correctly, understand when to give zinc tablets, and recognize danger signs requiring referral
techniques.southAsianContext: Diarrhea remains a leading cause of under-5 mortality in South Asia. ORS knowledge is high but correct preparation and use rates are low. Zinc co-packaging with ORS improved both practices. Private sector pharmacists often recommend antibiotics instead. ASHA-led ORS-zinc distribution programs show impact. Must address both home management and referral for severe cases.
implementationTips: Practice ORS preparation, don't just explain it
Distribute ORS-zinc kits proactively before rainy season
Engage private pharmacists as BCC channel
Teach danger sign recognition for timely referral

caseStudies.title: ORS-Zinc with Nutrition Counseling in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In Bara district, Female Community Health Volunteers combined ORS-zinc promotion with nutrition counseling, teaching mothers to continue breastfeeding during diarrhea episodes, offer zinc-rich foods during recovery, and use ORS preparation with safe water. The integrated approach addressed both treatment and the nutrition-diarrhea cycle.
caseStudies.outcome: ORS-zinc use during diarrhea increased from 28% to 67%; continued breastfeeding during diarrhea improved from 54% to 83%; post-diarrhea nutritional recovery was 35% faster in intervention areas.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from USAID Suaahara and CB-IMNCI integration in Nepal

techniques.name: Clean Cooking Fuel Adoption
techniques.definition: Promote transition from solid biomass fuels to LPG or other clean cooking fuels through awareness of health impacts, subsidy access, and supply chain reliability
techniques.example: Ujjwala Yojana beneficiary household transitioning from chulha to LPG through subsidy connection, followed by cooking demonstrations and regular refill support
techniques.southAsianContext: PM Ujjwala Yojana provided 9+ crore LPG connections. Key challenge is sustained use - many households revert to biomass due to refill cost, supply gaps, or cooking preference. Indoor air pollution causes 1+ million deaths annually in India. BCC must address economic barriers, cooking taste preferences, and supply chain issues alongside health messaging.
implementationTips: Address refill affordability, not just initial connection
Demonstrate cooking quality parity with LPG
Ensure reliable supply chain for refills
Frame as women's health and time savings

techniques.name: Maternal Health Continuum of Care
techniques.definition: Ensure behavior change messaging covers the full maternal health continuum: antenatal care, institutional delivery, postnatal care, and newborn care
techniques.example: ASHA accompanying a pregnant woman through ANC registration, birth preparedness planning, facility-based delivery, and three postnatal home visits, providing stage-appropriate counseling throughout
techniques.southAsianContext: NHM's continuum of care model addresses fragmentation where women attend ANC but deliver at home, or deliver at facility but miss postnatal care. ASHA's role spans the entire continuum. JSY incentivizes institutional delivery. HBNC (Home-Based Newborn Care) covers the postnatal period. LaQshya improves quality at delivery points. Each transition is a potential dropout point.
implementationTips: Track women across the continuum, not just single touchpoints
Address barriers at each transition point
Train ASHAs for the full continuum role
Involve family decision-makers at each stage

techniques.name: Breastfeeding Promotion and Support
techniques.definition: Multi-level support for early and exclusive breastfeeding through counseling, family engagement, and workplace/community enabling environments
techniques.example: BFHI-accredited hospital ensuring skin-to-skin contact within first hour, followed by ASHA home visits supporting breastfeeding technique and addressing family pressures for top-feeding
techniques.southAsianContext: India's exclusive breastfeeding rate is ~55% (NFHS-5). Key barriers: early supplementation pressure from family, workplace return, and marketing of formula/animal milk. MAA (Mothers' Absolute Affection) programme promotes optimal breastfeeding. Breastfeeding peer counselors and mother support groups are effective. Must address mother-in-law influence and workplace policies.
implementationTips: Provide immediate post-delivery support at facility
Engage mothers-in-law as breastfeeding supporters
Create mother support groups for ongoing peer help
Address specific breastfeeding problems, not just general promotion

caseStudies.title: Peer Counselor Breastfeeding Support in Bangladesh
caseStudies.description: In Chittagong division, trained peer counselors from the community provided home-based breastfeeding support through pregnancy and the first 6 months postpartum, with visits timed around common challenge points: initiation, day 3-5 engorgement, return to work at 40 days, and the 5-6 month complementary feeding transition.
caseStudies.outcome: Early initiation of breastfeeding within 1 hour increased from 36% to 73%; exclusive breastfeeding at 6 months improved from 42% to 74%; the peer model was sustained at lower cost than facility-based support.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from icddr,b and BRAC peer counselor breastfeeding trials, Bangladesh

techniques.name: Anemia Reduction Through Behavioral Approaches
techniques.definition: Promote iron-rich dietary practices, IFA supplementation adherence, and deworming uptake through multi-pronged BCC
techniques.example: AWW conducting cooking demonstration with iron-rich local foods (amaranth, jaggery, green leafy vegetables) while distributing IFA tablets and explaining importance of weekly supplementation
techniques.southAsianContext: India's anemia burden is massive - 57% of women, 67% of children under 5 (NFHS-5). Anemia Mukt Bharat programme targets through IFA supplementation, deworming, dietary diversification, and delayed cord clamping. Key behavioral barriers: IFA side effects (nausea), taste aversion, and food beliefs about iron-rich foods. Test-and-treat approach using HemoCue improves counseling specificity.
implementationTips: Address IFA side effects proactively in counseling
Demonstrate local iron-rich recipes
Use hemoglobin testing as biofeedback motivation
Target adolescent girls as key population

caseStudies.title: Adolescent Girl Anemia Reduction in India
caseStudies.description: In Madhya Pradesh's Mandla district, a multi-pronged behavioral approach to adolescent anemia combined weekly IFA supplementation with dietary counseling, cooking demonstrations using iron-rich local foods (amaranth, garden cress seeds, ragi), deworming, and menstrual health education through school-based platforms.
caseStudies.outcome: Anemia prevalence among adolescent girls decreased from 73% to 48% over 18 months; the combined behavioral-supplementation approach was more effective than IFA distribution alone, with dietary behavior changes contributing to sustained improvement.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Anemia Mukt Bharat and SABLA scheme evaluations, Madhya Pradesh

techniques.name: NCD Risk Factor Management
techniques.definition: Address behavioral risk factors for non-communicable diseases through screening, lifestyle counseling, and self-management support
techniques.example: Population-based screening at HWC (Health and Wellness Centre) identifying hypertensive individuals, providing dietary counseling, physical activity guidance, and medication adherence support
techniques.southAsianContext: India's NCD burden is rising rapidly - HWCs are the primary platform for NCD screening and management under Ayushman Bharat. Population-based screening for diabetes, hypertension, and cancers. Behavioral risk factors (tobacco, diet, physical inactivity, alcohol) require sustained behavior change. Community-based NCD management reduces hospital burden. Yoga promotion through International Yoga Day.
implementationTips: Screen and counsel at same visit (one-stop approach)
Provide simple lifestyle modification guidance
Create peer support groups for chronic disease management
Use digital tools for self-monitoring

techniques.name: Smoke-Free and Tobacco-Free Environments
techniques.definition: Create tobacco-free zones in public spaces, schools, and workplaces to reduce exposure, denormalize tobacco use, and support cessation
techniques.example: District administration declaring all government buildings, schools, and health facilities as tobacco-free zones under COTPA with visible signage and enforcement
techniques.southAsianContext: India's COTPA mandates smoke-free public places. Tobacco-free educational institutions initiative by MoHFW. Challenge: gutka/smokeless tobacco use is harder to police than smoking. State-level gutka bans (most states) require enforcement. Tobacco Free Village initiatives show community-led enforcement works. India's tobacco burden includes beedi, gutka, khaini, and hookah.
implementationTips: Enforce existing COTPA provisions
Create tobacco-free school zones
Support cessation alongside restriction
Engage community in enforcement, not just authorities

techniques.name: Kangaroo Mother Care Promotion
techniques.definition: Promote skin-to-skin contact between mother and newborn for preterm/low birth weight babies through training, family counseling, and facility-based initiation
techniques.example: Special Newborn Care Unit initiating KMC for preterm baby, training mother in technique, then ASHA ensuring continuation at home through daily visits during first week
techniques.southAsianContext: India has high preterm birth and LBW rates. KMC reduces neonatal mortality by 40%+ but adoption remains low. Barriers include family resistance, mother's workload, and lack of facility-based initiation. Comprehensive Newborn Care centres provide initiation. Community-based KMC for stable LBW babies is expanding. Must involve family members to share the holding burden.
implementationTips: Initiate at facility before discharge
Train family members to share KMC
Provide follow-up support through home visits
Address cultural barriers to prolonged skin-to-skin contact

categories.name: Livelihood & Economic Behavior
categories.description: Techniques addressing economic decision-making, livelihood diversification, financial behaviors, and market participation

techniques.name: Financial Literacy Campaigns
techniques.definition: Build knowledge and skills for managing money, savings, credit, and financial products through structured learning programs
techniques.example: SHG members participating in 7-session financial literacy module covering budgeting, savings calculation, loan comparison, and insurance understanding
techniques.southAsianContext: PMJDY and NRLM include financial literacy components. RBI's NSFE (National Strategy for Financial Education) targets grassroots education. SHGs are the primary platform for women's financial literacy. Digital financial literacy increasingly important with UPI proliferation. Must address both knowledge and confidence - women often know less but are more careful with money.
implementationTips: Deliver through SHG platforms using practical exercises
Include digital payment literacy
Use real money exercises, not just theory
Address gender-specific financial decision-making barriers

techniques.name: Crop Diversification Adoption
techniques.definition: Promote shift from monocropping to diversified cropping systems through demonstration, economic evidence, and market linkage
techniques.example: Farmer field school demonstrating intercropping of millets with pulses alongside traditional paddy, showing comparable income with lower water and input costs
techniques.southAsianContext: India's National Food Security Mission promotes crop diversification. Millet Mission revives nutritious traditional grains. Climate change makes diversification urgent. Barriers include MSP availability (mostly for paddy and wheat), market infrastructure, and risk aversion. Success stories from millet promotion in Odisha, Karnataka, and Rajasthan show the way.
implementationTips: Demonstrate economic viability through field trials
Ensure market linkage for diversified crops
Start with partial diversification, not complete switch
Connect to nutrition benefits for household consumption

caseStudies.title: Nutrition-Sensitive Agriculture and Diet Diversity in Nepal
caseStudies.description: In Kailali district, the KISAN II program promoted homestead food production of bio-fortified orange-fleshed sweet potato, iron-rich beans, and vitamin A-rich pumpkin varieties alongside nutrition education, linking crop diversification directly to improved complementary feeding for young children.
caseStudies.outcome: Households adopting nutrition-sensitive crops showed 47% higher dietary diversity scores; children under 5 consumed vitamin A-rich foods 2.3 more days per week; the agriculture-nutrition linkage was most effective when combined with cooking demonstrations.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from USAID KISAN II nutrition-sensitive agriculture program, Nepal

techniques.name: Insurance Uptake Promotion
techniques.definition: Increase enrollment in crop, health, and life insurance through simplified products, trust-building, and claims facilitation
techniques.example: Community-level awareness camp for PM Fasal Bima Yojana with simplified claim filing demonstration and testimonials from farmers who received payouts
techniques.southAsianContext: PMFBY for crop insurance, PMJJBY and PMSBY for life/accident insurance, Ayushman Bharat for health insurance. Low uptake despite subsidized premiums due to trust deficit, complex processes, and delayed claims. Weather-indexed insurance reduces moral hazard. Micro-insurance through SHGs improves reach. Claims experience (positive or negative) is the strongest predictor of renewal.
implementationTips: Simplify enrollment and claims processes
Share positive claims payout testimonials
Use CSC for assisted enrollment
Build trust through reliable claims settlement

techniques.name: Enterprise Behavior Development
techniques.definition: Build entrepreneurial mindset and behaviors in SHG members and rural youth through structured enterprise development programs
techniques.example: NRLM's community enterprise development program where SHG members progress from savings to internal lending to group enterprise (mushroom cultivation) to market linkage
techniques.southAsianContext: NRLM supports SHG enterprise development. MUDRA loans target micro-enterprises. Skill India Mission builds vocational capacity. Start-Up Village Entrepreneurship Programme (SVEP) supports rural enterprises. Key behavioral shifts: risk calculation, market orientation, quality consciousness, and financial management. Women's enterprises face additional barriers of mobility, time, and social norms.
implementationTips: Build enterprise skills progressively, not all at once
Provide mentoring alongside training
Create market linkages before enterprise launch
Address gender-specific enterprise barriers

techniques.name: Migration Decision Support
techniques.definition: Provide information and support for safe, informed migration decisions rather than discouraging migration, addressing both sending and receiving contexts
techniques.example: Pre-departure orientation for migrant workers from Bihar going to construction sites in Delhi, covering rights, safety, registration, and remittance channels
techniques.southAsianContext: Internal migration in India involves 139+ million people. Cross-border migration to Gulf countries from India, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka is massive. Migrants face exploitation, unsafe conditions, and loss of entitlements. Rather than anti-migration messaging, programs now support safe migration decisions, rights awareness, and portable social protection.
implementationTips: Provide pre-departure orientation on rights and services
Create destination-based support networks
Ensure portability of entitlements (PDS, health, education)
Support families left behind with services access

techniques.name: Savings Group Formation and Maintenance
techniques.definition: Facilitate formation and sustained operation of community savings groups as platforms for financial behavior change and mutual support
techniques.example: Facilitating formation of a 15-member SHG in an unreached hamlet, training in bookkeeping, and supporting weekly meetings until the group can function independently
techniques.southAsianContext: India's SHG-bank linkage model is the world's largest microfinance program. NRLM targets universal SHG coverage. Bangladesh pioneered savings-led microfinance through BRAC and Grameen. Savings behavior is foundational - regular saving builds financial discipline, group trust, and economic resilience. Community Resource Persons (CRPs) from mature SHGs help form new ones.
implementationTips: Use CRP model - experienced SHG members form new groups
Emphasize savings discipline before credit access
Ensure transparent bookkeeping from day one
Build toward federation for institutional sustainability

techniques.name: Cooperative and FPO Formation
techniques.definition: Build collective economic action through farmer producer organizations and cooperatives that enable better market access and bargaining power
techniques.example: Small dairy farmers forming a cooperative under the Amul model, pooling milk production, negotiating better prices, and accessing cold chain infrastructure collectively
techniques.southAsianContext: India's cooperative movement (Amul, IFFCO, KRIBHCO) demonstrates transformative potential. 10,000 FPOs target under government initiative. FPOs enable small farmers to access markets, credit, and technology collectively. Key behaviors: trust in collective action, transparent governance, and regular participation. Federation structures enable scale.
implementationTips: Build trust through transparent governance from day one
Start with activities that show immediate economic benefit
Invest in leadership development within the cooperative
Connect with market players for sustainable linkages

techniques.name: Market Linkage Behaviors
techniques.definition: Build farmer and producer behaviors for successful market participation including quality standards, aggregation, negotiation, and digital market access
techniques.example: Training FPO members to grade and sort produce to market specifications, use eNAM (electronic national agriculture market) for price discovery, and negotiate with buyers collectively
techniques.southAsianContext: Indian farmers typically receive 15-25% of final consumer price. eNAM, APMC reforms, and FPO support aim to improve market access. Agricultural produce marketing requires new behaviors - quality grading, aggregation, market information use, and collective negotiation. PM-KISAN Samman Nidhi can be leveraged for investment in quality inputs.
implementationTips: Build market literacy alongside production skills
Create market information systems accessible via phone
Support collective marketing through FPOs
Connect with corporate buyers for assured market

categories.name: Education & Child Development
categories.description: Techniques for improving educational behaviors, parental engagement, foundational learning, and child development outcomes

techniques.name: Parental Engagement in Learning
techniques.definition: Build parents' capacity and motivation to support children's learning at home through structured guidance and materials
techniques.example: Pratham's Read India program training mothers to spend 20 minutes daily reading with children using simple story cards provided at AWC
techniques.southAsianContext: Pratham's model demonstrates massive learning gains through parental engagement. ASER evidence shows home learning environments matter more than school inputs for foundational learning. Challenges include parental literacy levels, time poverty (especially mothers), and belief that education is solely the school's responsibility. Low-cost materials and simple activities can bridge the gap.
implementationTips: Provide simple, low-literacy-friendly learning materials
Set achievable daily time commitment (15-20 minutes)
Include fathers in learning engagement
Use community volunteers for parent coaching

techniques.name: School Enrollment and Retention Campaigns
techniques.definition: Community-level campaigns promoting school enrollment, regular attendance, and retention through awareness, incentives, and barrier removal
techniques.example: Village-level school enrollment drive during admission season with Panchayat members going door-to-door to ensure every 6-year-old is enrolled, with RTE rights awareness
techniques.southAsianContext: RTE Act guarantees free education but implementation gaps remain. Out-of-school children concentrated among SCs, STs, Muslims, girls, and children with disabilities. Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan supports access campaigns. Mid-Day Meal and school uniform distribution incentivize enrollment. Seasonal migration disrupts attendance. Bridge courses help re-enroll dropouts.
implementationTips: Target specific out-of-school populations
Address gender-specific barriers to enrollment
Provide bridge courses for re-enrollment
Involve School Management Committees actively

techniques.name: Foundational Literacy and Numeracy (FLN) Habits
techniques.definition: Build daily reading and math practice habits in children through structured activities, games, and family involvement
techniques.example: NIPUN Bharat-aligned program where children practice 10 minutes of reading and 10 minutes of math daily using activity cards that parents check off
techniques.southAsianContext: NEP 2020 prioritizes FLN through NIPUN Bharat. ASER evidence shows learning crisis - many children in Grade 5 cannot read Grade 2 text. TaRL (Teaching at the Right Level) by Pratham is proven effective. Learning camps during school holidays maintain gains. Practice at home is essential supplement to school instruction. Digital learning tools (apps, audio stories) expanding reach.
implementationTips: Focus on practice, not just instruction
Use TaRL approach for grade-appropriate learning
Create daily learning routines at home
Use learning games and activities, not worksheets alone

techniques.name: Adolescent Life Skills Education
techniques.definition: Build critical thinking, decision-making, communication, and socio-emotional skills in adolescents through structured curricula
techniques.example: Udaan program for adolescent girls covering financial literacy, reproductive health, gender rights, negotiation skills, and career planning through weekly group sessions
techniques.southAsianContext: Life skills education gaining traction through SABLA/Kishori Shakti, Adolescence Education Programme (AEP), and NGO programs like Quest Alliance, Breakthrough, and Room to Read. Critical for navigating pressures around early marriage, substance use, peer relationships, and career choices. Gender-specific curricula needed for girls and boys. Must go beyond knowledge to build practical skills.
implementationTips: Deliver through trained peer facilitators
Include both girls and boys (separately and together)
Go beyond knowledge to skill practice
Create safe spaces for sensitive topic discussion

techniques.name: School Management Committee Activation
techniques.definition: Strengthen school governance by building capacity of parent-community committees to monitor school quality and demand accountability
techniques.example: Training SMC members to conduct quarterly school quality assessments, review attendance data, and present findings to Block Education Officer
techniques.southAsianContext: RTE mandates School Management Committees but most are non-functional. When activated, SMCs improve teacher attendance, infrastructure maintenance, and learning outcomes. PAISA studies show SMC members often don't know their rights. Training on RTE provisions, school development planning, and community monitoring is essential. Women and marginalized community representation matters.
implementationTips: Train on RTE rights and SMC responsibilities
Provide simple monitoring tools
Ensure representation of marginalized communities
Create interface meetings with education officials

techniques.name: Anti-Child Labor and Child Protection
techniques.definition: Address child labor through awareness of laws, alternative livelihood support for families, and school enrollment drives
techniques.example: NCLP (National Child Labour Project) identifying working children in brick kilns, enrolling them in bridge education, and providing family livelihood support to replace lost income
techniques.southAsianContext: India banned child labor (under 14) in all occupations via 2016 amendment. Despite legal prohibition, children work in agriculture, domestic service, brick kilns, and informal sector. Poverty is the primary driver - BCC alone is insufficient without economic alternatives. Bachpan Bachao Andolan and NCPCR work on rescue and rehabilitation. Village-level Child Protection Committees are key structures.
implementationTips: Address economic drivers alongside awareness
Provide income alternatives for families
Use bridge education for transition to formal school
Strengthen village child protection committees

techniques.name: Early Childhood Stimulation
techniques.definition: Promote responsive caregiving and early stimulation behaviors that support cognitive, language, and socio-emotional development in children under 3
techniques.example: Training mothers and fathers in responsive feeding, play-based interaction, and language-rich communication with infants using locally available materials like pots, stones, and fabrics
techniques.southAsianContext: Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) is evolving from food supplementation to include early childhood stimulation. Lancet's Nurturing Care Framework adopted by India through National ECCE Policy. Gaps between knowledge and practice are large - many caregivers don't realize that talking, playing, and reading to infants builds brain architecture. Must include fathers.
implementationTips: Demonstrate with locally available play materials
Show fathers their role in early stimulation
Integrate into ICDS platform at AWC
Use home visits for personalized coaching

techniques.name: Digital Learning for Rural Youth
techniques.definition: Provide digital literacy and online learning opportunities for rural youth to build skills, access information, and participate in the digital economy
techniques.example: CSC-based digital literacy center where youth learn basic computer skills, internet navigation, government portal usage, and online financial transactions
techniques.southAsianContext: Digital India, PMGDISHA (digital literacy mission), and CSC network aim to bridge the digital divide. Youth digital literacy enables access to government services, financial products, market information, and educational content. Gender digital divide is significant - boys access phones and internet more than girls. Digital skills are increasingly essential for livelihoods.
implementationTips: Provide equal access for girls and young women
Use CSC infrastructure for training delivery
Focus on practical skills (UPI, government portals)
Create peer learning networks for ongoing support

categories.name: Climate & Environment
categories.description: Techniques for promoting climate-adaptive behaviors, environmental conservation, and sustainable resource management

techniques.name: Climate-Adaptive Farming Practices
techniques.definition: Promote adoption of farming practices that build resilience to climate variability including drought, flood, and temperature extremes
techniques.example: Farmer field school demonstrating drought-resistant millet varieties, mulching techniques, and rainwater harvesting structures in semi-arid Rajasthan
techniques.southAsianContext: South Asia is among the most climate-vulnerable regions. NABARD's watershed development programs, NICRA (National Innovations on Climate Resilient Agriculture), and state adaptation plans promote climate-smart agriculture. Barriers include high upfront costs, uncertain returns, and preference for familiar practices. Demonstrations showing economic viability are key.
implementationTips: Demonstrate economic viability alongside climate benefits
Start with low-cost, low-risk adaptations
Use farmer-to-farmer learning
Link to government subsidies and support schemes

techniques.name: Waste Segregation and Management
techniques.definition: Promote household-level waste segregation and responsible disposal through awareness, infrastructure provision, and community systems
techniques.example: Ward-level door-to-door collection with color-coded bins (green for wet, blue for dry) supported by community awareness campaign and regular collection schedule
techniques.southAsianContext: SBM 2.0 focuses on solid and liquid waste management. Urban ULBs mandated to implement segregation. Rural waste management through GOBARDHAN (biogas from cattle dung) and community composting. Rag picker integration into formal waste systems. Key challenge is sustaining segregation behavior over time. User fees for collection improve sustainability.
implementationTips: Provide easy-to-use segregation infrastructure
Establish reliable collection schedules
Create economic incentives for segregation
Integrate informal waste workers into formal systems

caseStudies.title: Solid Waste Management and Drain Cleaning for WASH in India
caseStudies.description: In Odisha's Bhubaneswar slums, community-based waste segregation programs linked solid waste management to improved drainage and reduced waterlogging, framing waste segregation as both environmental and WASH behavior to prevent monsoon-season disease outbreaks.
caseStudies.outcome: Participating communities showed 42% reduction in monsoon-season diarrheal disease; drain blockages decreased by 67%, reducing stagnant water breeding sites for mosquitoes.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Bhubaneswar Municipal Corporation community WASH initiatives

techniques.name: Water Conservation Behaviors
techniques.definition: Promote water-saving practices in agriculture and household use through awareness, technology, and community water management
techniques.example: Community-managed water budgeting exercise where village maps water sources, calculates available water, and collectively decides cropping patterns to match supply
techniques.southAsianContext: Jal Shakti Abhiyan and Atal Bhujal Yojana promote water conservation. Community-managed water budgets in Maharashtra's watershed programs. Micro-irrigation adoption through PMKSY. Traditional water harvesting revival (johads, tankas, baoris). Key behaviors: crop-water matching, micro-irrigation, rainwater harvesting, and groundwater recharge. Social regulation of borewells is challenging but essential.
implementationTips: Use community water budgeting as starting point
Promote micro-irrigation with subsidy support
Revive traditional water harvesting knowledge
Create water user associations for governance

caseStudies.title: Groundwater Recharge and Safe Water Conservation in Sri Lanka
caseStudies.description: In Jaffna peninsula communities facing both water scarcity and salinity intrusion, a dual WASH-conservation program promoted rainwater harvesting for drinking water safety alongside groundwater recharge pits, framing water conservation as both an environmental and health behavior.
caseStudies.outcome: Household rainwater harvesting adoption reached 64% in intervention areas; families using harvested rainwater showed 52% lower prevalence of kidney-related complaints associated with high-salinity groundwater.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from UNDP Sri Lanka water security and WASH programs, Northern Province

techniques.name: Renewable Energy Uptake
techniques.definition: Promote adoption of solar, biogas, and other renewable energy sources through awareness, financing, and demonstration
techniques.example: Solar study lamp distribution to school children in off-grid villages, demonstrating solar technology while improving study hours and reducing kerosene expenditure
techniques.southAsianContext: PM-KUSUM for solar pumps, solar study lamp programs, GOBARDHAN for biogas, PM Ujjwala for LPG transition. Solar micro-grids in remote areas. Key barrier is upfront cost despite long-term savings. Subsidy programs and EMI-based models improve affordability. Solar pump adoption growing rapidly due to free electricity and reduced diesel dependency.
implementationTips: Demonstrate economic savings alongside environmental benefits
Provide financing/EMI options for upfront costs
Train local technicians for maintenance
Use early adopters as demonstration sites

techniques.name: Disaster Preparedness Behaviors
techniques.definition: Build community and household preparedness behaviors for floods, cyclones, earthquakes, and other climate-related disasters
techniques.example: Mock drill in coastal Odisha village where families practice cyclone evacuation, first aid, and emergency supply preparation, reducing response time during actual Cyclone Fani
techniques.southAsianContext: NDMA's community-based disaster risk reduction programs. Odisha's cyclone preparedness is a global success story - mortality dropped from 10,000+ (1999 Super Cyclone) to 64 (2019 Fani) through community preparedness. Task forces, early warning systems, evacuation plans, and practice drills save lives. Flood-prone areas in Bihar, Assam, and Bangladesh need similar sustained efforts.
implementationTips: Conduct regular mock drills
Create village disaster management committees
Ensure early warning reaches the last mile
Build on traditional knowledge of disaster signals

techniques.name: Biodiversity Conservation Behaviors
techniques.definition: Promote community-led biodiversity conservation through awareness of ecological connections, traditional conservation practices, and sustainable use
techniques.example: Tribal community reviving sacred grove (devvan) traditions alongside modern biodiversity documentation, creating community-managed conservation areas recognized under FRA
techniques.southAsianContext: Sacred groves across India demonstrate indigenous conservation traditions. Forest Rights Act empowers tribal communities for forest management. Community-based natural resource management (CBNRM) programs. Biodiversity Management Committees under Biological Diversity Act. Joint Forest Management (JFM) engages communities in conservation. Economic alternatives to destructive resource extraction are essential.
implementationTips: Build on existing indigenous conservation traditions
Ensure economic alternatives to destructive use
Support community rights over natural resources
Link conservation to livelihoods (eco-tourism, NTFP)

techniques.name: Crop Residue Management
techniques.definition: Promote alternatives to crop residue burning through awareness of health/environmental impacts and subsidized access to management technologies
techniques.example: Demonstration of Happy Seeder technology for zero-till wheat sowing directly into paddy stubble, showing comparable yields without burning in Punjab
techniques.southAsianContext: Stubble burning in Punjab-Haryana causes severe air pollution in North India. Despite NGT ban, burning continues due to short turnaround time between paddy harvest and wheat sowing. Happy Seeder, mulching, and in-situ decomposition are alternatives. CRM machines subsidized under SMAM. Behavioral barriers: cost, access to machinery, and traditional practice preference.
implementationTips: Provide subsidized access to CRM machinery
Demonstrate yield parity with residue management
Create community-level machinery banks for sharing
Use social pressure from neighbors affected by smoke

techniques.name: Natural Resource Management Behaviors
techniques.definition: Promote sustainable use of common pool resources (forests, water, grazing lands) through community-based governance and conservation practices
techniques.example: Community establishing rotational grazing rules for common pastures, with penalties for overgrazing, monitored by village natural resource committee
techniques.southAsianContext: India's JFM, NTFP management under FRA, watershed development programs, and community fisheries management address commons governance. Key behaviors: compliance with harvesting rules, participation in conservation activities, and reporting violations. Success depends on genuine community ownership and benefit sharing. Elinor Ostrom's principles apply directly.
implementationTips: Ensure equitable benefit sharing from conservation
Build on traditional resource management rules
Create community monitoring mechanisms
Support legal recognition of community rights (FRA)

categories.name: Governance & Accountability
categories.description: Techniques for promoting citizen engagement, government scheme access, transparency, and democratic participation

techniques.name: RTI Usage Promotion
techniques.definition: Build community capacity to use the Right to Information Act as a tool for accessing information and ensuring government accountability
techniques.example: Training SHG members to file RTI applications about MGNREGA fund utilization in their panchayat, revealing discrepancies between sanctioned and actual works
techniques.southAsianContext: RTI Act 2005 is a powerful accountability tool but usage remains concentrated in urban, educated populations. RTI training for grassroots communities can transform governance. Satark Nagrik Sangathan and NCPRI provide RTI literacy. Key barriers: complex filing process, fear of retaliation, and slow response. Online RTI portals have improved access somewhat.
implementationTips: Simplify RTI filing process through facilitated support
Create RTI resource persons in each village
Follow up on applications to ensure responses
Build collective RTI filing for systemic issues

techniques.name: Government Scheme Awareness and Access
techniques.definition: Systematically inform communities about available government schemes and support enrollment through facilitation and simplification
techniques.example: Scheme mela (fair) at block level where all department representatives are present, community members learn about eligible schemes, and enrollment is completed on-the-spot
techniques.southAsianContext: India has 700+ government schemes but awareness and access remain poor, especially among marginalized populations. Jan Sampark (public outreach), CSC-based enrollment, and gram sabha-based awareness drives address the gap. Key schemes needing BCC: PMJAY, PM-KISAN, MGNREGA, PMFBY, Jan Dhan, Ujjwala. Digital India makes online access possible but digital divide persists.
implementationTips: Organize scheme melas at accessible locations
Use local language IEC materials
Provide facilitation support for enrollment
Track actual benefit receipt, not just enrollment

techniques.name: Voter Awareness and Political Participation
techniques.definition: Promote informed voter participation through awareness of electoral rights, candidate platforms, and democratic processes
techniques.example: SVEEP (Systematic Voters' Education and Electoral Participation) campaign using local artists, school children, and SHG members to promote informed voting
techniques.southAsianContext: India's Election Commission SVEEP program is the world's largest voter education effort. Low participation among women, youth, urban poor, and migrants. ECI uses innovative BCC - campus ambassadors, accessibility for PwD voters, and transgender inclusion. Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka have similar electoral awareness challenges. Must maintain non-partisan approach.
implementationTips: Maintain strict non-partisan approach
Target low-participation demographics specifically
Use peer education through campus ambassadors
Address accessibility barriers for PwD and elderly voters

techniques.name: Panchayat Participation and Local Governance
techniques.definition: Build citizen capacity to participate in local governance through Gram Sabha attendance, engagement with elected representatives, and community monitoring
techniques.example: Ward-level preparatory meetings where community members identify priority issues and select representatives to present them at the upcoming Gram Sabha
techniques.southAsianContext: 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendments devolved power to panchayats, but citizen participation remains low. Women's reservation (33-50%) has increased representation but not always voice. SC/ST/OBC reservation has similar challenges. Gram Sabha attendance is typically low. Programs that build pre-Sabha preparation, facilitate women's participation, and follow up on decisions show impact.
implementationTips: Organize pre-Gram Sabha preparation meetings
Support women and marginalized groups to participate
Create follow-up mechanisms for decisions
Build capacity of elected representatives

techniques.name: MGNREGA Rights Awareness
techniques.definition: Build awareness of MGNREGA entitlements including 100 days guaranteed work, minimum wages, worksite facilities, and unemployment allowance
techniques.example: Rights-based awareness campaign where landless laborers learn to demand work through written application, triggering the guarantee mechanism and ensuring timely wage payment
techniques.southAsianContext: MGNREGA is the world's largest employment guarantee but demand-driven nature means awareness is critical. Many eligible households don't apply for work. Worksite facilities (shade, water, crèche) are often absent. Wage delays undermine participation. Social audits improve accountability. Azim Premji Foundation and LibTech India support MGNREGA governance.
implementationTips: Focus on demand generation through written applications
Train communities on worksite facility entitlements
Support social audit processes
Create awareness of grievance redressal mechanisms

techniques.name: PDS Entitlement Awareness
techniques.definition: Ensure beneficiary awareness of Public Distribution System entitlements, quality standards, and complaint mechanisms
techniques.example: Community members trained to check their ration card eligibility online, verify correct quantity and quality of rations, and use the grievance portal for shortages
techniques.southAsianContext: PDS reaches 800+ million people through NFSA. One Nation One Ration Card enables portability for migrants. Common issues: quantity shortfalls, quality problems, and dealer malpractice. ePoS machines have improved transparency. Beneficiary awareness of exact entitlements (5 kg/person/month) and complaint mechanisms is essential for accountability.
implementationTips: Display entitlements prominently at fair price shops
Train beneficiaries to verify quantity using phone
Create awareness of complaint mechanisms
Support migrant workers to use ONORC

caseStudies.title: PDS Entitlement Awareness for Nutrition Security in India
caseStudies.description: In Chhattisgarh's Bastar region, community mobilizers helped tribal families understand and access their full Public Distribution System entitlements including fortified rice, pulses, and edible oil under the National Food Security Act, linking PDS utilization to improved household dietary diversity.
caseStudies.outcome: Full PDS entitlement uptake increased from 48% to 82%; households accessing complete PDS rations showed 31% higher dietary diversity; the intervention was most impactful for female-headed households and Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups.
caseStudies.source: Adapted from Right to Food Campaign and NFSA implementation studies, Chhattisgarh

techniques.name: Citizen Feedback and Grievance Mechanisms
techniques.definition: Strengthen citizen capacity to provide feedback on services and file grievances through accessible channels
techniques.example: Training women SHG members to use the CPGRAMS portal or state grievance helpline to report non-functional hand pumps, with follow-up tracking
techniques.southAsianContext: CPGRAMS, CM helplines, and Jan Sunwai platforms provide formal grievance channels. Social media-based grievance redressal growing. Key challenge: feedback loops - citizens file complaints but rarely get resolution, leading to disengagement. Programs that support the full cycle from complaint to resolution demonstrate that participation works.
implementationTips: Train on both filing and follow-up processes
Track resolution rates to maintain community engagement
Use group/collective grievances for systemic issues
Connect to media and civil society for amplification

techniques.name: Budget Literacy and Tracking
techniques.definition: Build community capacity to understand, track, and influence local government budget allocations and expenditures
techniques.example: Training community members to read the Gram Panchayat budget, track expenditure under 14th Finance Commission grants, and present discrepancy findings at Gram Sabha
techniques.southAsianContext: Budget transparency is improving through eGramSwaraj portal and PFMS. But community capacity to analyze and use budget data remains low. Budget tracking by CSOs like CBGA and social accountability programs builds citizen oversight. When communities understand budget flows, they can advocate for priority allocations and detect leakage.
implementationTips: Simplify budget data into visual formats
Train community budget monitors
Present budget analysis at Gram Sabha
Connect budget tracking to service delivery outcomes

techniques.name: Citizen Report Cards
techniques.definition: Systematically collect citizen feedback on public services through surveys and present findings to service providers and policy makers for accountability
techniques.example: Public Affairs Centre conducting citizen report card on urban water supply in Bangalore, publishing results that led to improved service delivery by BWSSB
techniques.southAsianContext: Public Affairs Centre in Bangalore pioneered citizen report cards in India. Now used for health services, education, PDS, water supply, and municipal services. Creates evidence-based advocacy for service improvement. When published through media, creates public pressure. Works best when repeated periodically to track improvement over time.
implementationTips: Use random sampling for representative data
Present findings to both citizens and service providers
Track changes over multiple rounds
Combine with media engagement for pressure
